questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with bilateral knee pain for 8 months, worse with climbing stairs and after prolonged sitting. She has a BMI of 33 and no history of trauma. Exam reveals crepitus, mild medial joint line tenderness, and small bilateral effusions. Range of motion is slightly limited but symmetric. The MOST appropriate initial management includes:

options.A: Order bilateral MRI for meniscal tear evaluation
options.B: Prescribe naproxen 500 mg BID, initiate physical therapy, and counsel on weight loss
options.C: Refer to orthopedic surgery for evaluation of joint replacement
options.D: Aspirate the knee effusions and send for culture and crystal analysis

questions.rationale: This presentation is classic for bilateral knee osteoarthritis (OA): older woman, overweight, insidious bilateral pain, crepitus, limited ROM, no fever or systemic symptoms. First-line management is exercise therapy, weight loss, and analgesics (NSAIDs or acetaminophen). MRI (A) is not indicated as a first step for a clinical diagnosis of OA — imaging is done with X-ray if needed, and MRI is reserved for suspected structural injury in surgical candidates. Orthopedic referral (C) is premature before a trial of conservative therapy; surgical evaluation would be appropriate after failure of 6-12 months of conservative management. Aspiration (D) is indicated only when septic arthritis is suspected (fever, acute monoarthritis, warm joint disproportionate to history) — bilateral painless effusions with OA presentation do not warrant aspiration.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: MRI is not the appropriate first imaging step for classic OA. X-ray is appropriate and OA remains a clinical diagnosis in most cases.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Orthopedic referral is reserved for failure of adequate conservative management, not at initial presentation.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Aspiration is not indicated for typical bilateral OA effusions. Aspiration is needed when septic arthritis is suspected.

questions.keyConceptTested: First-line management of knee OA: PT + weight loss + NSAIDs vs. premature escalation

questions.stem: A 38-year-old male with a history of gout presents to urgent care with a 24-hour history of severe right knee pain, swelling, and warmth. He has had two prior gout attacks affecting the right first metatarsophalangeal joint. Temperature is 38.4°C, HR 96. The right knee is visibly swollen, warm, and the patient refuses passive ROM due to pain. Serum uric acid is 7.2 mg/dL. What is the MOST critical next step?

options.A: Start colchicine 0.6 mg BID for presumed gout flare
options.B: Prescribe indomethacin and arrange follow-up in 72 hours
options.C: Perform or urgently arrange arthrocentesis of the right knee for culture and crystal analysis
options.D: Order X-ray of the right knee and check ESR and CRP

questions.rationale: Fever (38.4°C) with acute monoarthritis makes septic arthritis a life-threatening consideration that CANNOT be excluded clinically, even in a patient with established gout. Gout and septic arthritis can coexist. The only way to distinguish septic arthritis from crystal-induced arthritis is joint aspiration with WBC, Gram stain, culture, and crystal analysis. A normal uric acid level does not exclude gout, and a history of gout does not exclude infection. Treating empirically as gout (A or B) without ruling out infection risks missed septic joint, joint destruction, and sepsis. X-ray and inflammatory markers (D) are useful adjuncts but neither rules in nor rules out septic arthritis — they do not replace arthrocentesis.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Treating empirically for gout when fever is present is dangerous — septic arthritis must be ruled out first.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Same error — presuming gout without excluding infection. A 72-hour delay in septic arthritis treatment causes irreversible joint damage.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: X-ray and ESR/CRP are supportive but cannot distinguish gout from septic arthritis. Arthrocentesis is mandatory.

questions.keyConceptTested: Septic arthritis cannot be excluded clinically in acute monoarthritis with fever — arthrocentesis is mandatory

questions.stem: A 24-year-old female college athlete reports anterior knee pain that worsens when walking downstairs, sitting in a movie theater for 2 hours, and squatting. There is no history of injury. Exam shows tenderness with patellar compression (Clarke's test positive) and mild crepitus. No effusion. The MOST likely diagnosis and appropriate first-line treatment are:

options.A: Meniscal tear — MRI and orthopedic referral
options.B: Patellofemoral pain syndrome — quadriceps strengthening physical therapy and activity modification
options.C: Anterior cruciate ligament sprain — knee brace and orthopedic referral
options.D: Prepatellar bursitis — aspiration and corticosteroid injection

questions.rationale: The combination of anterior knee pain worsened by stairs, prolonged sitting (the 'movie sign'), and squatting in a young active woman with no injury, positive Clarke's test, and no effusion is classic patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS). First-line treatment is VMO-targeted quadriceps strengthening physical therapy plus activity modification. MRI and orthopedic referral (A) are not indicated for a clinical diagnosis of PFPS without red flags. ACL sprain (C) presents with acute injury mechanism, instability, and significant effusion — not matching this presentation. Prepatellar bursitis (D) presents with localized swelling over the anterior patella, typically in patients who kneel repetitively — no effusion over patella is described here.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: No injury, no effusion, and classic PFPS pattern — MRI is unnecessary and would not change initial management.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: ACL injury requires a traumatic mechanism and produces significant instability and effusion.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Prepatellar bursitis is localized swelling anterior to the patella; this patient has diffuse anterior knee pain without bursitis features.

questions.keyConceptTested: Patellofemoral pain syndrome clinical diagnosis and first-line PT management

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with new right knee pain. He has no knee injury but notes his left hip has been bothering him for months with stiffness and reduced internal rotation. On right knee exam, there is no effusion, normal ROM, and no joint line tenderness. Hip exam on the right reveals restricted internal rotation and mild groin pain. What is the MOST likely explanation for the right knee pain?

options.A: Early-stage right knee osteoarthritis
options.B: Referred pain from right hip pathology
options.C: L3-L4 lumbar radiculopathy
options.D: Prepatellar bursitis related to occupational kneeling

questions.rationale: Hip pathology commonly refers pain to the knee, medial thigh, and groin. When a knee exam is entirely normal but the hip shows restricted internal rotation and pain — hip osteoarthritis should be the primary diagnosis. This is why the clinical rule exists: ALWAYS examine the hip in any knee complaint. The history of bilateral hip involvement with stiffness and the normal knee exam strongly support referred pain from the hip. L3-L4 radiculopathy (C) is possible but presents with pain in a dermatomal pattern from the spine downward, typically with associated back pain and positive femoral nerve stretch test — not isolated knee pain with normal spine exam. Early knee OA (A) would show crepitus, joint line tenderness, or limited knee ROM.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Normal knee exam (no effusion, full ROM, no tenderness) makes knee OA very unlikely as the primary pain source.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: L3-L4 radiculopathy would show dermatomal distribution, positive femoral stretch, and associated spinal findings.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: No occupational kneeling history and the hip findings make bursitis an incorrect conclusion.

questions.keyConceptTested: Hip-to-knee referred pain — always examine the hip in knee complaints; normal knee exam with hip findings points to referred pain

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes and HTN presents with acute onset right knee pain over 12 hours. He recalls no injury. The knee is swollen, erythematous, and warm. Temperature 37.0°C. You aspirate the joint and send for analysis. Results show WBC 18,000/mm³ with 70% neutrophils and rhomboid-shaped crystals that are positively birefringent under polarized microscopy. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.A: Refer to ED for orthopedic irrigation and systemic antibiotics
options.B: Start colchicine 0.6 mg BID and arrange follow-up in 1 week
options.C: Order serum uric acid and start allopurinol immediately
options.D: Order synovial fluid culture and prescribe empiric ceftriaxone pending results

questions.rationale: Positively birefringent rhomboid-shaped crystals under polarized light = calcium pyrophosphate crystals = pseudogout (CPPD disease). This is distinct from gout (negatively birefringent needle-shaped urate crystals). WBC 18,000 with neutrophilia is consistent with crystal-induced arthritis and does not confirm infection — the diagnostic confirmation here is the crystal analysis. Management of acute pseudogout is identical to acute gout: colchicine, NSAIDs, or corticosteroids. Uric acid (C) is irrelevant for pseudogout and allopurinol has no role in CPPD. Option D would be appropriate for septic arthritis, not crystal-confirmed pseudogout. Option A (ED/antibiotics) is not indicated with a confirmed non-infectious diagnosis.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Crystal confirmation changes the diagnosis — this is pseudogout, not septic arthritis requiring surgical washout.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Serum uric acid and allopurinol are treatments for gout (urate crystals), not CPPD.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Once crystals are identified, bacterial infection has been excluded as the primary diagnosis — empiric antibiotics without culture evidence of infection is inappropriate.

questions.keyConceptTested: Distinguishing pseudogout (CPPD: positive birefringent, rhomboid) from gout (negatively birefringent, needle-shaped); management of pseudogout

questions.stem: You are applying the Ottawa Knee Rules to decide whether to order knee X-ray for a 47-year-old man who twisted his knee stepping off a curb. He can bear weight (walked 4 steps in the office) but limps. There is isolated tenderness over the fibular head but no other bony tenderness. Which Ottawa Knee Rule criterion is present that justifies ordering the X-ray?

options.A: Age ≥55
options.B: Isolated patella tenderness
options.C: Fibular head tenderness
options.D: Inability to flex knee to 90 degrees

questions.rationale: The Ottawa Knee Rules specify 5 criteria; presence of ANY ONE justifies X-ray: (1) age ≥55, (2) isolated patella tenderness, (3) fibular head tenderness, (4) inability to flex to 90°, (5) inability to bear weight for 4 steps immediately after injury AND in the ED/clinic. This patient has fibular head tenderness (criterion 3), which is sufficient to indicate X-ray regardless of his ability to bear weight. The other options listed are indeed Ottawa criteria, but they are not present in this specific scenario: he is 47 (not ≥55), has no patellar tenderness, and can flex the knee (not described as limited to <90°).

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Patient is 47, not ≥55 — this criterion is not met in this scenario.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: No patellar tenderness is described in this scenario.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Ability to flex to 90° is not stated as limited in this scenario.

questions.keyConceptTested: Ottawa Knee Rules — fibular head tenderness as an independent indication for X-ray

questions.stem: A 27-year-old non-pregnant healthy woman presents with 2 days of dysuria, urinary frequency, and suprapubic discomfort. She has no fever, no flank pain, and no prior UTI in the past year. Urinalysis shows pyuria and positive nitrites. She has no known drug allergies. Which antibiotic regimen is the MOST appropriate first-line choice?

options.A: Ciprofloxacin 250 mg BID x 3 days
options.B: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals (Macrobid) 100 mg BID x 5 days
options.C: Amoxicillin 500 mg TID x 7 days
options.D: Cephalexin 500 mg QID x 7 days

questions.rationale: Nitrofurantoin (Macrobid) 100 mg BID x 5 days is the preferred first-line agent for uncomplicated cystitis per IDSA guidelines due to its high efficacy, excellent safety profile, and minimal collateral damage to gut flora. It maintains low resistance rates compared to TMP-SMX in many regions. Ciprofloxacin (A) is not recommended for uncomplicated UTI — fluoroquinolones should be reserved for complicated UTI and pyelonephritis; using them for simple cystitis contributes to fluoroquinolone resistance and carries risks of tendinopathy and Clostridioides difficile. Amoxicillin (B) has high E. coli resistance rates (>30-50%) and is not recommended. Cephalexin (D) is reasonable for complicated UTI but is not a first-line choice for uncomplicated cystitis.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Fluoroquinolones should be reserved for complicated UTI — using them for uncomplicated cystitis is a stewardship violation.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Amoxicillin has high resistance rates for uropathogens and is not a recommended UTI treatment.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Cephalexin is acceptable for some UTI scenarios but is a broader-spectrum option than needed here and not the first-line choice.

questions.keyConceptTested: First-line antibiotic selection for uncomplicated UTI — nitrofurantoin preferred over fluoroquinolones

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman at 16 weeks gestation presents with dysuria and frequency. Urinalysis shows pyuria. Urine culture from last week's prenatal visit grew >100,000 CFU/mL of E. coli, but she was asymptomatic at the time and was not treated. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate management?

options.A: Reassure her that asymptomatic bacteriuria does not require treatment in pregnancy
options.B: Prescribe nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID x 5 days after confirming current culture
options.C: Prescribe trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS BID x 3 days
options.D: Prescribe ciprofloxacin 250 mg BID x 3 days

questions.rationale: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy MUST be treated — this is one of the few exceptions to the 'do not treat ASB' rule. Untreated ASB in pregnancy progresses to pyelonephritis in 20-40% of cases and is associated with preterm labor and low birth weight. Now that she is symptomatic, this is an active UTI in pregnancy requiring treatment. Nitrofurantoin is safe in the 2nd trimester (avoid at ≥36 weeks due to risk of neonatal hemolytic anemia). Obtain a current culture to guide therapy given the prior result. TMP-SMX (C) should be avoided in the first trimester (folate antagonist causing neural tube defects) and near term (kernicterus risk) — at 16 weeks it could be used but is not the preferred agent. Ciprofloxacin (D) is contraindicated in pregnancy (theoretical fetal cartilage damage). Option A is incorrect — ASB in pregnancy is always treated.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy requires treatment — failure to treat risks pyelonephritis and preterm labor.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: TMP-SMX is avoided in the first trimester (folate antagonism) and near term; at 16 weeks it is used in some guidelines but nitrofurantoin is the safer and more universally recommended choice.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Fluoroquinolones are contraindicated in pregnancy.

questions.keyConceptTested: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy requires treatment; pregnancy-safe antibiotic selection

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman in a skilled nursing facility is noted by staff to have 'smelly urine' and altered mental status (AMS) over the past 2 days. A catheterized urinalysis shows pyuria and bacteriuria (>100,000 CFU/mL E. coli on prior culture). She has no fever, no dysuria (incontinent at baseline), no flank pain, and no change in urinary complaints. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.A: Start nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID x 5 days — bacteriuria in elderly is always treated
options.B: Evaluate and treat for alternative causes of AMS; hold antibiotics unless infection clearly confirmed
options.C: Start ciprofloxacin 250 mg BID x 3 days — fluoroquinolones penetrate tissue better in elderly
options.D: Repeat urinalysis with straight catheterization and treat any growth >100,000 CFU/mL

questions.rationale: This is a classic stewardship scenario: bacteriuria is extremely common in older women (>50% prevalence in nursing homes) and does not equal urinary tract infection. The criteria for treating UTI require SYMPTOMS — in non-catheterized patients: dysuria, frequency, urgency; in catheterized patients: fever, rigors, AMS with no other source, new costovertebral tenderness. Bacteriuria + AMS does not automatically equal UTI — AMS in elderly patients commonly results from medication changes, dehydration, constipation, early dementia fluctuation, or other infections. Treating with antibiotics exposes this patient to Clostridioides difficile risk, resistance selection, and adverse drug effects without confirmed benefit. A thorough evaluation for other AMS causes is required. Repeat cultures (D) of a catheterized patient will always grow bacteria — culture cannot diagnose UTI in the absence of symptoms.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Bacteriuria in elderly should NOT be routinely treated without symptoms — this is one of the most common inappropriate antibiotic prescribing scenarios.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Fluoroquinolones are not first-line for any uncomplicated UTI scenario; tissue penetration does not justify their use here.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Repeating a catheter culture without treating underlying symptoms does not differentiate colonization from infection.

questions.keyConceptTested: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in elderly: do not treat. AMS alone does not confirm UTI in nursing home patients.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with Type 2 diabetes (A1c 9.2%) presents with 3 days of dysuria, frequency, and right flank pain. Temperature is 38.6°C, HR 102. Urinalysis: large leukocyte esterase, positive nitrites, WBC casts present. CrCl is 78 mL/min. Blood cultures are drawn. Which combination of the following is the MOST appropriate management?

options.A: Nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID x 5 days plus close follow-up in 48 hours
options.B: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID x 7 days with urine culture obtained before starting antibiotics; return precautions for worsening
options.C: Cephalexin 500 mg QID x 3 days — adequate for diabetic patients with pyelonephritis
options.D: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS BID x 3 days after culture results

questions.rationale: This patient has acute pyelonephritis complicated by diabetes (complicating factor): fever, tachycardia, flank pain, WBC casts (pathognomonic for upper tract involvement). She can tolerate oral medication (no vomiting described, vitals concerning but not septic shock). Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID x 7 days is appropriate first-line oral treatment for pyelonephritis in an outpatient who can be managed without admission. Culture MUST be obtained before starting antibiotics. Hospital admission should be strongly considered given her diabetes and severity — if she can maintain oral hydration and close follow-up, outpatient treatment is an option with clear return precautions. Nitrofurantoin (A) does NOT achieve adequate renal tissue concentrations and is NEVER appropriate for pyelonephritis. Cephalexin (C) 3 days is inadequate for pyelonephritis; 10-14 days minimum if cephalosporin used. TMP-SMX (D) should be avoided empirically for pyelonephritis unless local resistance <10% and cultures return susceptible — starting it empirically without culture guidance risks treatment failure.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Nitrofurantoin does not achieve therapeutic tissue concentration in the kidney — it is contraindicated for pyelonephritis.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: 3 days of cephalexin is inadequate for pyelonephritis — 7-14 days required with the appropriate agent.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Do not hold antibiotics until culture results when patient has pyelonephritis — treat empirically with appropriate agent.

questions.keyConceptTested: Pyelonephritis: requires renal-tissue-penetrating antibiotic (ciprofloxacin); nitrofurantoin contraindicated; culture before antibiotics

questions.stem: A 64-year-old postmenopausal woman reports her fourth UTI in the past 12 months. She is not sexually active. All prior cultures grew E. coli sensitive to nitrofurantoin and TMP-SMX. She had another culture-confirmed UTI last week and was treated successfully. Which prevention strategy is MOST evidence-based for this patient?

options.A: Daily oral cranberry tablets indefinitely
options.B: Continuous low-dose nitrofurantoin prophylaxis (50-100 mg nightly) for 6-12 months
options.C: Methenamine hippurate 1 g BID — appropriate for all recurrent UTI prevention
options.D: Topical vaginal estrogen cream applied twice weekly

questions.rationale: In postmenopausal women, vaginal atrophy from estrogen deficiency disrupts the normal vaginal flora (lactobacillus predominance), raising vaginal pH and increasing colonization with uropathogens. Topical vaginal estrogen restores the protective flora and has the strongest evidence for recurrent UTI prevention in postmenopausal women — it is the preferred first-line non-antibiotic prevention strategy. Systemic absorption is minimal, making it safe for most postmenopausal women. Continuous antibiotic prophylaxis (B) is also evidence-based and is the alternative if topical estrogen is contraindicated or insufficient; however, vaginal estrogen is recommended first in postmenopausal women. Cranberry (A) has weak and inconsistent evidence — it is a reasonable adjunct but not first-line prevention. Methenamine hippurate (C) requires adequate urinary acidification to work and has limited evidence quality in most populations.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Cranberry tablets have modest and inconsistent evidence — not first-line for postmenopausal recurrent UTI prevention.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Antibiotic prophylaxis is an option but carries resistance and C. diff risk; vaginal estrogen is preferred non-antibiotic prevention in postmenopausal women.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Methenamine is not recommended as first-line recurrent UTI prevention in this population.

questions.keyConceptTested: Postmenopausal recurrent UTI: topical vaginal estrogen as first-line evidence-based prevention strategy

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with 4 days of dysuria and urinary frequency. He also reports perineal pressure and pain. On exam, the prostate is tender and boggy on digital rectal examination. Temperature 38.1°C. What is the MOST appropriate treatment regimen?

options.A: Nitrofurantoin 100 mg BID x 5 days — first-line for all male UTIs
options.B: Fosfomycin 3 g single dose — effective for male lower tract infection
options.C: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID x 4-6 weeks
options.D: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole DS BID x 7 days

questions.rationale: The presentation (dysuria, fever, perineal pain, tender boggy prostate) is acute bacterial prostatitis, not simple cystitis. Prostatitis requires an antibiotic that achieves excellent prostate tissue penetration. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) and TMP-SMX are the two agents with proven prostate penetration, but fluoroquinolones are preferred first-line. The CRITICAL distinction: prostatitis requires 4-6 weeks of therapy (NOT 3-7 days) to prevent chronic prostatitis and abscess formation. Nitrofurantoin (A) does not achieve adequate prostate tissue concentration — it concentrates in urine, not tissue. Fosfomycin single dose (B) is appropriate only for uncomplicated cystitis. TMP-SMX (D) has adequate prostate penetration but 7 days is far too short for prostatitis.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Nitrofurantoin does not penetrate prostate tissue — inappropriate for prostatitis.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Single-dose fosfomycin is for uncomplicated cystitis only — inadequate for prostatitis requiring weeks of therapy.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: TMP-SMX can treat prostatitis but 7 days is insufficient — 4-6 weeks required.

questions.keyConceptTested: Acute bacterial prostatitis: fluoroquinolone for 4-6 weeks; nitrofurantoin insufficient due to lack of tissue penetration

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with T2DM (A1c 8.4%) on metformin 1000 mg BID has established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (prior MI 3 years ago, on aspirin, statin, ACE inhibitor). eGFR is 62 mL/min/1.73m². He asks about adding a second agent to improve his A1c and cardiovascular risk. Which agent is the BEST addition to his regimen?

options.A: Glipizide 5 mg daily — effective A1c lowering and low cost
options.B: Semaglutide (Ozempic) 0.25 mg SC weekly, titrating to target dose — ASCVD benefit plus A1c lowering
options.C: Sitagliptin 100 mg daily — cardiovascular safety established and weight-neutral
options.D: Pioglitazone 15 mg daily — improves insulin sensitivity with cardiovascular benefit

questions.rationale: For a patient with established ASCVD, current guidelines (ADA 2024, ACC/AHA) recommend a GLP-1 receptor agonist (GLP-1 RA) or SGLT-2 inhibitor as the preferred second agent due to proven cardiovascular outcome benefits beyond glycemic control. Semaglutide (SUSTAIN-6 trial) and liraglutide (LEADER trial) have demonstrated significant reduction in MACE in patients with established ASCVD. eGFR 62 is adequate for semaglutide use. Glipizide (A) is inexpensive and lowers A1c but carries significant hypoglycemia risk and provides no cardiovascular benefit — not preferred in ASCVD. Sitagliptin (C) is cardiovascular-safe (TECOS trial: neutral) but provides no MACE reduction — it is an acceptable alternative but not the best choice in established ASCVD. Pioglitazone (D) has some CV benefit evidence but causes fluid retention and weight gain, which could worsen heart failure — not preferred in this post-MI patient.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Glipizide lowers A1c but no CV benefit; hypoglycemia risk is significant, especially in older adults.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Sitagliptin has neutral CV safety — it does not reduce MACE, making it inferior to GLP-1 RA in established ASCVD.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Pioglitazone has fluid retention risk and is not preferred post-MI without heart failure evaluation.

questions.keyConceptTested: ASCVD-driven drug selection in T2DM: GLP-1 RA preferred for proven MACE reduction

questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman with T2DM (A1c 8.1%), HFrEF (EF 35%), eGFR 41 mL/min, and no prior ASCVD is on metformin 500 mg BID (recently dose-reduced due to eGFR decline) and linagliptin 5 mg daily. She presents for medication review. You want to add an agent that addresses both glycemic control and her heart failure. Which is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Add saxagliptin 5 mg daily — additional DPP-4 inhibitor synergy
options.B: Add dapagliflozin 10 mg daily — SGLT-2 inhibitor with proven HFrEF mortality benefit
options.C: Start liraglutide 0.6 mg SC daily — GLP-1 RA with heart failure benefit
options.D: Start pioglitazone 15 mg daily — improves insulin sensitivity in CKD

questions.rationale: SGLT-2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) have proven HFrEF mortality and hospitalization reduction benefits (EMPEROR-Reduced and DAPA-HF trials) independent of diabetes status. Dapagliflozin is FDA-approved for HFrEF even without T2DM. With eGFR 41, glycemic efficacy diminishes, but the HF benefit and renal protective effect (DAPA-CKD) remain intact for eGFR ≥25. This makes dapagliflozin the ideal addition. Saxagliptin (A) is SPECIFICALLY CONTRAINDICATED in heart failure — it was associated with increased hospitalization for HF in the SAVOR-TIMI trial. Adding more DPP-4 inhibition (she already has linagliptin) is also irrational. Liraglutide (C) has demonstrated NEUTRAL to potentially harmful effects in patients with decompensated heart failure — GLP-1 RA is generally avoided in advanced HF. Pioglitazone (D) causes fluid retention and worsens heart failure — it is CONTRAINDICATED in HFrEF.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Saxagliptin specifically increases heart failure hospitalization risk — it is contraindicated in HFrEF.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: GLP-1 RA is not the preferred agent in HFrEF; SGLT-2 inhibitors have superior evidence in this indication.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Pioglitazone causes sodium and fluid retention — it is contraindicated in heart failure.

questions.keyConceptTested: HFrEF + T2DM: SGLT-2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin) preferred; saxagliptin and pioglitazone contraindicated in HF

questions.stem: A 73-year-old man with T2DM (A1c 7.8%), atrial fibrillation on apixaban, CKD stage 3a (eGFR 52), mild cognitive impairment, and multiple prior falls is on metformin 500 mg BID. His daughter is concerned about hypoglycemia. You plan to add a second agent with low hypoglycemia risk and minimal cognitive/fall burden. Which is the BEST choice?

options.A: Glimepiride 1 mg daily — low dose minimizes hypoglycemia risk in elderly
options.B: Linagliptin 5 mg daily — no renal dose adjustment, no hypoglycemia risk, minimal drug interactions
options.C: Empagliflozin 10 mg daily — SGLT-2 inhibitor preferred in all T2DM patients over 65
options.D: Semaglutide oral 3 mg daily — GLP-1 RA with lowest GI burden

questions.rationale: In a frail older adult with cognitive impairment, falls, CKD, and anticoagulation, hypoglycemia prevention is paramount. Linagliptin is the ideal choice: no renal dose adjustment required (important for eGFR 52 and further decline), no intrinsic hypoglycemia risk (glucose-dependent mechanism), weight-neutral, minimal drug interactions (not metabolized by CYP3A4 significantly), and no orthostatic effects. DPP-4 inhibitors are particularly well-suited to frail elderly. Glimepiride (A) — all sulfonylureas cause hypoglycemia, which in this patient with cognitive impairment and fall risk is extremely dangerous; low-dose does not eliminate this risk. Empagliflozin (C) — SGLT-2 inhibitors cause volume depletion and increased urination, worsening fall risk from orthostatic hypotension and increasing the complexity of managing anticoagulation with volume changes; eGFR 52 limits glycemic efficacy (though CKD/HF benefits remain). Oral semaglutide (D) requires specific empty-stomach administration instructions that a patient with cognitive impairment may not reliably follow; GI side effects can cause dehydration.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Sulfonylureas cause hypoglycemia — contraindicated by Beers Criteria in elderly with fall risk; especially glyburide, but all SU carry this risk.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Volume depletion from SGLT-2 inhibitors worsens fall risk and orthostatic hypotension in a frail elder on anticoagulation.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Oral semaglutide requires strict empty-stomach administration that is not reliable in cognitive impairment; GI effects create dehydration risk.

questions.keyConceptTested: Frail elder T2DM: linagliptin preferred — no renal adjustment, no hypoglycemia, no fall risk; sulfonylurea Beers Criteria avoidance

questions.stem: You initiate empagliflozin 10 mg daily in a 52-year-old woman with T2DM (A1c 8.3%) and obesity (BMI 34). Three weeks later, she calls reporting severe nausea, abdominal pain, and fatigue. Her home glucose meter shows 142 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows large ketones. Which condition is MOST likely, and what is the appropriate initial action?

options.A: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) — glucose is too low; reassure and advise dietary change
options.B: Euglycemic DKA related to empagliflozin — stop the medication and send to ED immediately
options.C: Hypoglycemia — have her eat a snack and recheck glucose in 30 minutes
options.D: UTI with ketones — send urinalysis culture and prescribe nitrofurantoin

questions.rationale: Euglycemic DKA is a well-documented but often missed complication of SGLT-2 inhibitors. The hallmark: significant ketonemia with NORMAL or only mildly elevated blood glucose (unlike classic DKA with glucose >250 mg/dL). SGLT-2 inhibitors shift metabolic substrate toward fat oxidation, suppress insulin, and increase glucagon — creating ketosis without necessarily causing marked hyperglycemia. The triad of nausea, abdominal pain, and fatigue plus large urinary ketones in a patient on an SGLT-2 inhibitor = euglycemic DKA until proven otherwise. Stop empagliflozin immediately and send to ED for serum ketones, blood gas, and IV fluids. Normal glucose (A) does not rule out DKA in this context — this is the classic diagnostic pitfall. There is no hypoglycemia (C) — glucose is 142. UTI (D) does not explain large ketonuria or the systemic symptoms in this context.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Normal glucose does NOT rule out DKA on SGLT-2 inhibitors — euglycemic DKA is a real and serious complication.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Glucose of 142 is not hypoglycemic; the ketones and systemic symptoms indicate a metabolic emergency.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: The clinical picture is not consistent with UTI as the primary explanation — large ketonuria and systemic symptoms require DKA workup.

questions.keyConceptTested: Euglycemic DKA: SGLT-2 inhibitor complication — normal glucose does not exclude DKA; ketones + abdominal pain = emergency

questions.stem: A 47-year-old woman newly diagnosed with T2DM has an A1c of 10.8% and reports polyuria, polydipsia, and 15 lb weight loss over 2 months. She is otherwise hemodynamically stable and has no nausea, vomiting, or neurological symptoms. eGFR 88, no ASCVD, BMI 31. What is the MOST appropriate initial strategy?

options.A: Start metformin 500 mg BID; titrate over 4-8 weeks; recheck A1c in 3 months
options.B: Start metformin + semaglutide 0.25 mg SC weekly; schedule for 3-month follow-up
options.C: Start basal insulin (glargine 10 units nightly) + metformin; assess for possible insulin deficiency vs Type 1 DM
options.D: Refer to endocrinology before initiating any treatment

questions.rationale: An A1c of 10.8% with symptomatic hyperglycemia (polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss) indicates significant beta-cell stress and potential insulin deficiency. ADA guidelines recommend that patients with A1c ≥10% or symptomatic hyperglycemia may need initial combination therapy including insulin to rapidly correct glucose toxicity. Basal insulin reduces glucotoxicity quickly, which can itself restore some beta-cell function. Metformin should be added concurrently. It is also critical to consider whether this could be late-onset Type 1 DM (LADA) — measure GAD65 antibodies and C-peptide; if LADA, insulin is essential. Starting metformin alone (A) would take 3-6 months to achieve meaningful A1c reduction — unacceptable with symptomatic hyperglycemia at 10.8%. Metformin + GLP-1 RA (B) is appropriate for patients at lower A1c levels — the GLP-1 RA's slower titration is not rapid enough for severe hyperglycemia. Endocrinology referral (D) is appropriate as a co-management step but should not delay initiation of treatment.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Metformin monotherapy with a 3-month delay is too slow for A1c 10.8% with symptomatic hyperglycemia.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: GLP-1 RA requires months of dose titration — insufficient for rapid glycemic correction in severe hyperglycemia.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Initiate treatment now; referral can happen concurrently but should not delay necessary therapy.

questions.keyConceptTested: A1c ≥10% with symptomatic hyperglycemia: combination therapy including insulin required; avoid monotherapy delay

questions.stem: Which of the following patients with T2DM has the MOST appropriate individualized A1c target of 8.0%?

options.A: A 45-year-old healthy woman with T2DM x 2 years, no complications, on metformin only
options.B: A 78-year-old man with T2DM, stage 4 CKD, early Alzheimer's dementia, recurrent falls, on 4 medications
options.C: A 60-year-old man with T2DM on metformin and empagliflozin, A1c currently 7.8%
options.D: A 52-year-old woman with T2DM and recent MI, A1c 8.1%, on semaglutide

questions.rationale: ADA standards specify that A1c targets should be individualized. For a frail older adult (78 years) with multiple comorbidities (stage 4 CKD, Alzheimer's dementia causing impaired hypoglycemia awareness, recurrent falls, and polypharmacy), a less stringent target of 7.5-8.0% is appropriate. The primary concern shifts to hypoglycemia prevention and quality of life rather than microvascular complication prevention. Tight control risks severe hypoglycemia with falls, which can be life-threatening. Option A: a healthy, younger patient with uncomplicated T2DM should target <7.0%. Option C: current A1c 7.8% is nearly at target — no change to 8.0% is needed. Option D: post-MI patient on semaglutide — ASCVD risk is the driver; target <7.0% to maximize cardiovascular benefit while semaglutide already reduces MACE risk.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Healthy young patient with uncomplicated T2DM — target <7.0%.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: A1c 7.8% on dual therapy is reasonably controlled — no indication to raise the target.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Post-MI, the priority is maximizing GLP-1 RA cardiovascular benefit while achieving good glycemic control — target <7.0%.

questions.keyConceptTested: Individualized A1c targets in older adults: frailty, cognitive impairment, falls = 7.5-8.0% acceptable; avoid hypoglycemia

questions.stem: A patient with T2DM and CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38) needs intensification of therapy. She is already on metformin 500 mg daily (dose-reduced) and is not at A1c goal. Her last serum creatinine and eGFR were checked 4 months ago. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.A: Add canagliflozin 100 mg daily — SGLT-2 inhibitors are approved for all CKD stages
options.B: Recheck eGFR, then if stable consider adding linagliptin or a GLP-1 RA; discuss SGLT-2 inhibitor for renal protection if eGFR ≥25
options.C: Stop metformin immediately — contraindicated with eGFR <45
options.D: Add glipizide 5 mg daily — safe in CKD and inexpensive

questions.rationale: Monitoring eGFR before making prescribing decisions in CKD is essential — CKD can progress quickly. With eGFR 38 (stage 3b): metformin can continue cautiously at reduced doses down to eGFR 30 (hold if <30). SGLT-2 inhibitors: glycemic benefit diminishes at eGFR <45 but renal and cardiovascular protective effects persist to eGFR ≥25 (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin for CKD indication). GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, liraglutide) are safe across all eGFR stages. Linagliptin requires NO renal dose adjustment. Canagliflozin (A) — while approved for CKD protection, it requires careful assessment of current eGFR before dosing decisions. Stopping metformin (C) at eGFR 38 is not yet required — guidelines say reduce/hold at eGFR 30-45, stop at <30. Glipizide (D) can cause hypoglycemia, which is particularly dangerous in CKD due to impaired gluconeogenesis and drug accumulation.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Partial answer — checking current eGFR is required before making final prescribing decisions; eGFR may have progressed.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Metformin is contraindicated at eGFR <30, not <45; at eGFR 38, it can continue at reduced dose with monitoring.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Sulfonylureas cause hypoglycemia, which is more dangerous in CKD; drug metabolites accumulate, increasing hypoglycemia duration.

questions.keyConceptTested: CKD + T2DM prescribing: recheck eGFR before changes; metformin thresholds; SGLT-2 renal protective effect persists to eGFR ≥25

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with COPD presents for management review. Post-bronchodilator spirometry shows FEV1/FVC 0.58 and FEV1 62% predicted. He has a CAT score of 14 and had ONE moderate exacerbation (required course of antibiotics and steroids) last year, not requiring hospitalization. He is currently on albuterol MDI PRN only. Blood eosinophils are 180 cells/μL. Which GOLD group is he in, and what is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.A: GOLD Group A — add SABA regularly scheduled
options.B: GOLD Group B — initiate LAMA monotherapy (e.g., tiotropium)
options.C: GOLD Group E — initiate LAMA + LABA dual bronchodilator therapy
options.D: GOLD Group B — initiate LAMA + ICS combination due to eosinophilia

questions.rationale: GOLD 2024 ABE classification: Group E = ≥2 moderate exacerbations OR ≥1 hospitalization/year. This patient had ONE moderate exacerbation (not hospitalized) = Group A or B based on symptoms. CAT ≥10 = more symptoms = Group B. Therefore: GOLD Group B. The recommended initial therapy for Group B is a single long-acting bronchodilator — preferably LAMA (LAMA has superior evidence over LABA alone in COPD). LAMA + LABA (C) would be appropriate if he had more symptoms or was in Group E. LAMA + ICS (D) is inappropriate — ICS is not added without Group E exacerbation history or blood eosinophils ≥300 (his eosinophils are 180, below the ICS threshold). Group A (A) designation requires CAT <10 or mMRC 0-1 — his CAT of 14 excludes Group A.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: CAT 14 (≥10) places this patient in Group B, not A. Scheduled SABA alone is not the appropriate long-term management.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: One exacerbation without hospitalization does not meet Group E criteria — LAMA monotherapy is appropriate initial therapy for Group B.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: ICS addition requires eosinophils ≥300 or Group E classification — blood eosinophils of 180 do not meet the ICS threshold.

questions.keyConceptTested: GOLD 2024 ABE classification: Group B = more symptoms + 0-1 uncomplicated exacerbations → LAMA monotherapy first-line

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with COPD (FEV1 38% predicted) on tiotropium + salmeterol/fluticasone combination inhaler presents with an acute exacerbation: increased dyspnea, green sputum, cough, and fever 38.2°C. SpO2 is 90% on room air. She is coherent and can maintain oral hydration. She is on inhaler therapy for COPD and albuterol PRN. Which of the following represents the MOST appropriate initial management for outpatient treatment of this exacerbation?

options.A: Add oral prednisone 40 mg daily x 5 days; add azithromycin 500 mg day 1 then 250 mg x 4 days; intensify albuterol; provide supplemental oxygen to target SpO2 88-92%; close follow-up in 24-48 hours
options.B: Admit to hospital for IV methylprednisolone and IV antibiotics — SpO2 90% mandates admission
options.C: Add oral prednisone 40 mg daily x 14 days; start ciprofloxacin 500 mg BID x 7 days
options.D: Add inhaled budesonide/formoterol extra doses — ICS is preferred over oral steroids for exacerbations

questions.rationale: This patient meets criteria for a moderate COPD exacerbation: worsening dyspnea, purulent sputum, fever. SpO2 90% with clear mentation and ability to maintain oral hydration supports outpatient management. Standard outpatient exacerbation treatment: prednisone 40 mg daily x 5 days (no additional benefit from longer courses — REDUCE trial), azithromycin (appropriate for moderate exacerbation in a patient likely with atypical organism contribution, no documented contraindication), intensified SABA, and supplemental O2 targeting SpO2 88-92% (avoid driving to >95% in chronic CO2 retainers). Option B — admission criteria not met: she is coherent, can tolerate orals, no severe hemodynamic compromise, no acute respiratory failure. Option C — 14-day prednisone course is NOT recommended (5-day course is evidence-based; longer does not add benefit and increases adverse effects). Option D — nebulized ICS is NOT equivalent to systemic corticosteroids for exacerbations; oral steroids are the standard of care.

incorrectOptionExplanations.B: SpO2 90% on room air alone does not mandate admission if the patient is alert, can take oral medications, and has reliable access to follow-up.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: 14-day steroid course is not evidence-based; 5 days is standard and equivalent in outcomes.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Inhaled ICS does not adequately treat exacerbations — systemic corticosteroids are required.

questions.keyConceptTested: COPD exacerbation outpatient management: prednisone 5 days, azithromycin, SABA intensification, oxygen target 88-92%

questions.stem: A 69-year-old man with COPD Group E (3 exacerbations in the past year) is on LAMA + LABA therapy. His blood eosinophil count is 420 cells/μL. He wants to avoid escalation due to medication costs. You prescribe fluticasone/umeclidinium/vilanterol (Trelegy Ellipta). He asks why you added the ICS. Which explanation is MOST clinically accurate?

options.A: ICS is added to all COPD patients on dual bronchodilators as a standard maintenance step
options.B: ICS is added because blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/μL AND Group E exacerbation frequency indicate he is an eosinophilic phenotype likely to derive anti-inflammatory benefit from ICS
options.C: Trelegy is selected only for COPD patients with concurrent asthma overlap — he must have mixed disease
options.D: ICS is added because he failed monotherapy; the exact eosinophil level does not influence the decision

questions.rationale: Blood eosinophil count is the key biomarker for ICS benefit in COPD. GOLD 2024 recommends considering ICS addition in COPD patients with: (1) blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/μL, and/or (2) Group E (frequent exacerbations) despite dual bronchodilators. This patient has BOTH: eosinophils 420 (≥300) AND Group E status — he is the ideal candidate for triple therapy. The IMPACT trial demonstrated that triple therapy (LAMA + LABA + ICS) significantly reduced exacerbations and all-cause mortality in patients with ≥1 exacerbation. ICS is NOT added universally to all COPD patients — it increases pneumonia risk and is specifically indicated based on eosinophil/exacerbation phenotype. Asthma overlap (C) is not the only indication.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: ICS is NOT standard for all COPD patients — it is added selectively based on eosinophilic phenotype and exacerbation frequency.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Trelegy is approved for COPD regardless of asthma overlap — eosinophilia and Group E status are the ICS indicators.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Blood eosinophil level directly influences the ICS decision — it is a validated biomarker for ICS responsiveness in COPD.

questions.keyConceptTested: ICS in COPD: indicated for blood eosinophils ≥300 AND/OR Group E — not universal; eosinophil-guided prescribing

questions.stem: Which of the following COPD management recommendations is INCORRECT according to current GOLD guidelines?

options.A: Post-bronchodilator spirometry showing FEV1/FVC <0.70 is required to diagnose COPD
options.B: Salmeterol (LABA) should be prescribed as monotherapy in COPD patients with significant symptoms
options.C: Pulmonary rehabilitation should be offered to patients with GOLD 2-4
options.D: Annual influenza vaccination is recommended for all COPD patients

questions.rationale: LABA monotherapy is NOT recommended as initial therapy in COPD — unlike asthma, where LABA is used with ICS as a controller agent. In COPD, if a single long-acting bronchodilator is initiated, a LAMA is preferred due to superior evidence for symptom control, exacerbation reduction, and FEV1 improvement. LABA monotherapy in COPD is generally avoided because LABA alone has inferior evidence to LAMA, and using LABA alone could also mislead management into bypassing LAMA benefits. Post-bronchodilator spirometry (A) is correctly required for diagnosis — clinical diagnosis alone is insufficient. Pulmonary rehabilitation (C) is correctly recommended for GOLD 2-4. Annual influenza vaccination (D) is a standard recommendation for all COPD patients.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Correct GOLD guideline — spirometry is required for diagnosis.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Correct — pulmonary rehabilitation is recommended for GOLD 2-4.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Correct — influenza vaccination is recommended for all COPD patients.

questions.keyConceptTested: LAMA preferred over LABA as single long-acting bronchodilator in COPD; LABA monotherapy not recommended

questions.stem: An 81-year-old woman with severe COPD (FEV1 28%) on triple inhaler therapy presents for a routine visit. Her daughter mentions her mother uses the Trelegy Ellipta inhaler incorrectly — she doesn't inhale forcefully enough through the DPI. She lives alone and has mild cognitive impairment. Which adjustment is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Switch to nebulized treatments for all bronchodilators and corticosteroids
options.B: Switch to a soft-mist inhaler (SMI) equivalent (Spiriva Respimat) and educate on slow deep breath technique; simplify regimen
options.C: Hospitalize for pulmonary rehabilitation training
options.D: Add oral theophylline to supplement inadequate inhaler delivery

questions.rationale: DPI (dry powder inhalers) require sufficient inspiratory flow — in severe COPD and frail older adults, the inspiratory force may be inadequate for reliable DPI delivery. Soft-mist inhalers (SMI, e.g., Respimat) generate a slow-moving aerosol that does not require high inspiratory flow, making them superior for patients with severe COPD or limited inspiratory effort. Simplifying the regimen (once-daily if possible) and providing family/caregiver education is appropriate. Nebulizers (A) are an alternative but add cost, time, and equipment burden — SMI is preferable as a first adaptation. Hospitalization (C) is not indicated for inhaler technique alone. Theophylline (D) has a narrow therapeutic index, significant drug interactions, and is not a first-line add-on in elderly patients — not appropriate here.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Nebulizers are an alternative but SMI is a more practical, cost-effective first-line switch for DPI technique failure.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Hospitalization is not indicated for inhaler technique issues — this can be addressed outpatient.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Theophylline's narrow therapeutic window and adverse effect profile make it inappropriate as an add-on in this frail older adult.

questions.keyConceptTested: Inhaler selection in frail elderly: DPI requires inspiratory flow — switch to soft-mist inhaler when technique inadequate

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with COPD presents with an acute exacerbation. Her SpO2 is 87% on room air. You start supplemental oxygen. What is the CORRECT SpO2 target for oxygen therapy in this patient?

options.A: 94-98% — standard target for all patients with respiratory symptoms
options.B: 100% — maximal oxygenation is always the safest target
options.C: 88-92% — targeted oxygen to avoid hypercapnic respiratory failure from blunted hypoxic drive
options.D: 85-88% — the lowest tolerable level to prevent oxygen toxicity

questions.rationale: In patients with COPD who are chronic CO2 retainers (known or suspected hypercapnia), the oxygen target is 88-92% SpO2. Driving SpO2 above 92-94% in these patients can suppress the hypoxic respiratory drive they depend on, leading to worsening hypercapnia and CO2 narcosis. Titrate oxygen (nasal cannula 1-2 L/min typically sufficient) to maintain 88-92%. Targeting 94-98% (A) is appropriate for most medical patients but can cause CO2 retention in COPD. Targeting 100% (B) is never a target — hyperoxia is harmful in COPD. 85-88% (D) is too low — permitting hypoxia below 88% is not a recommended strategy.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Standard target for non-COPD patients — dangerous in known/suspected CO2 retainers.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Hyperoxia in COPD can blunt hypoxic drive and cause CO2 retention — never target 100%.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: SpO2 below 88% is not a safe or recommended target — 88-92% is the appropriate lower boundary.

questions.keyConceptTested: Oxygen therapy in COPD: target SpO2 88-92%; avoid hyperoxia to prevent suppression of hypoxic drive and CO2 narcosis

questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with COPD presents for follow-up after 3 hospitalizations for exacerbations in the past year. He is on triple inhaler therapy (LAMA + LABA + ICS) and is a current smoker. His blood eosinophils are 380 cells/μL. What is the MOST impactful additional intervention to reduce his future exacerbation risk?

options.A: Add roflumilast 500 mcg daily — PDE-4 inhibitor for frequent exacerbators
options.B: Add azithromycin 250 mg three times weekly — prophylactic macrolide for chronic use
options.C: Refer urgently to pulmonology; intensify smoking cessation with pharmacotherapy; refer to pulmonary rehabilitation
options.D: Switch to a higher-dose ICS formulation

questions.rationale: Three hospitalizations in one year on maximal triple therapy while continuing to smoke represents a complex high-exacerbation phenotype. The highest-impact intervention is addressing modifiable risk: smoking cessation is the only intervention proven to slow FEV1 decline. Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise capacity and reduces exacerbations. Pulmonology referral is appropriate for a patient with frequent exacerbations on maximal therapy who may need phenotypic workup (alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, bronchiectasis, eosinophilic endotype, potential biologic candidates). Roflumilast (A) is appropriate adjunct for patients with chronic bronchitis phenotype (productive cough) on maximal therapy — a reasonable consideration but not the most impactful single intervention compared to smoking cessation + rehab. Azithromycin prophylaxis (B) is evidence-based for chronic macrolide use in frequent exacerbators — also reasonable but carries QTc and hearing risks; requires careful patient selection; secondary option. Higher ICS dose (D) has no evidence for reducing exacerbations beyond standard doses.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Roflumilast is a valid add-on for chronic bronchitis phenotype but is secondary to addressing smoking and rehabilitation.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Chronic azithromycin is a valid option but requires QTc and hearing monitoring and is not the most impactful step without addressing smoking.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Higher ICS dose without eosinophil-guided evidence does not reduce exacerbations and increases pneumonia risk.

questions.keyConceptTested: Frequent exacerbator COPD: smoking cessation most impactful; pulmonary rehab; escalation options (roflumilast, macrolide prophylaxis)

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman presents to your urgent care clinic reporting 'the worst headache of my life.' It began suddenly while she was exercising, reached maximum intensity within 60 seconds, and is now 9/10. She has no prior headache history. Neurological exam is normal. Temperature 37.0°C. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate IMMEDIATE next step?

options.A: Diagnose migraine — new onset migraine often presents severely and is exacerbated by physical activity
options.B: Order sumatriptan 6 mg SC to abort what is likely a migraine attack
options.C: Transfer to ED immediately for non-contrast CT head; if CT negative, proceed to lumbar puncture
options.D: Order MRI of the brain — more sensitive than CT for subarachnoid hemorrhage

questions.rationale: Thunderclap headache ('worst headache of my life,' reaching maximum intensity within seconds to minutes) is a SAH until proven otherwise. SAH is a neurosurgical emergency with 30-day mortality of 30-40%; those who survive often have severe deficits. The diagnostic algorithm: non-contrast CT head FIRST (90-98% sensitive within 6 hours of SAH onset); if CT is negative but clinical suspicion remains high (which it does here — classic thunderclap) → lumbar puncture to detect xanthochromia or persistent elevated RBCs that indicate SAH missed by CT. This patient MUST be transferred to the ED — she cannot be managed in urgent care. MRI (D) is less sensitive than CT for acute SAH (poor detection of subarachnoid blood in first 24 hours) and takes longer — CT is the correct first test. Migraine diagnosis (A and B) must never be made in the setting of a first thunderclap headache without exclusion of SAH.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: First thunderclap headache cannot be diagnosed as migraine without ruling out SAH — this is a potentially fatal error.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Administering a triptan without excluding SAH is inappropriate and dangerous in thunderclap headache.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: MRI is less sensitive than CT for acute subarachnoid hemorrhage and takes longer — CT is the preferred first imaging modality.

questions.keyConceptTested: Thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise: CT head first, then LP if CT negative — never treat as migraine without ruling out SAH

questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman presents with a new headache over the past 3 weeks, predominantly over the right temple. She reports jaw fatigue when chewing and scalp tenderness when combing her hair. ESR is 88 mm/hr, CRP is markedly elevated. What is the MOST appropriate immediate management?

options.A: Order temporal artery biopsy today; await results before starting steroids to avoid invalidating the biopsy
options.B: Start high-dose prednisone 40-60 mg daily TODAY; schedule temporal artery biopsy within 2 weeks
options.C: Refer to ophthalmology for urgent fundoscopy; start steroids only if papilledema is found
options.D: Order CTA of the head and neck; start aspirin 81 mg daily for presumed arteritis

questions.rationale: This is giant cell arteritis (GCA, temporal arteritis) until proven otherwise — a vascular inflammatory emergency where the primary threat is irreversible vision loss (anterior ischemic optic neuropathy). GCA presents in patients >50 with temporal headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, elevated ESR (>50 mm/hr), and elevated CRP. The cardinal rule: START HIGH-DOSE PREDNISONE IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for biopsy results. Temporal artery biopsy remains the gold standard diagnostic test but should be done within 2 weeks of starting steroids (histologic findings remain positive for up to 2-4 weeks on steroids). Delaying steroids to avoid 'invalidating the biopsy' (A) risks permanent blindness — the biopsy is NOT invalidated within 2 weeks of steroid initiation. Ophthalmology referral (C) is appropriate but not before starting steroids. CTA and aspirin (D) are adjuncts — treatment is systemic corticosteroids, not aspirin.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Never delay steroids waiting for biopsy in suspected GCA — vision loss can occur within hours to days.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Starting steroids should not be conditional on finding papilledema — GCA is a clinical diagnosis supported by labs.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: CTA can help diagnose large-vessel GCA but starting aspirin alone is not the treatment — high-dose steroids are required.

questions.keyConceptTested: GCA: start high-dose prednisone IMMEDIATELY before biopsy — do not delay treatment to preserve biopsy validity (remains valid for 2 weeks)

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with a 5-year history of migraines presents with a headache she describes as 'just my usual migraine' but more severe than typical. She has had no fever or stiff neck. Exam shows a right-sided pupil that is 6 mm and non-reactive to light. The left pupil is 3 mm and reactive. Which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.A: Treat as migraine with triptan — pupil asymmetry can occur in migraine with aura
options.B: Transfer to ED immediately for urgent CT angiography — asymmetric pupil suggests posterior communicating artery aneurysm
options.C: Check for prior Horner's syndrome in the chart and reassure if documented history
options.D: Refer to ophthalmology for next-week appointment for formal pupil assessment

questions.rationale: An isolated fixed dilated pupil (mydriasis) with a contralateral normal pupil and headache = posterior communicating artery (PComm) aneurysm compressing the oculomotor nerve (CN III) until proven otherwise. CN III compression by a PComm aneurysm preferentially affects the parasympathetic pupillomotor fibers (on the outer surface), causing ipsilateral mydriasis and ptosis. This is a sentinel warning sign of impending aneurysm rupture → subarachnoid hemorrhage. Even in a patient with known migraines, a new neurological sign (anisocoria with unreactive dilated pupil) is a SNOOP4 red flag mandating immediate CT angiography to rule out aneurysm. Treating as migraine (A) risks missing an about-to-rupture aneurysm. Horner's syndrome (C) causes a SMALL pupil (miosis) with partial ptosis, not a large dilated non-reactive pupil. Ophthalmology referral next week (D) is dangerously delayed.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Migraine with aura can cause visual changes but does NOT cause a fixed dilated pupil — this is a neurological emergency.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Horner's syndrome causes miosis (small pupil), not mydriasis (large pupil) — opposite finding.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: A fixed dilated pupil in the context of headache is an emergency — next-week referral is unacceptably delayed.

questions.keyConceptTested: Fixed dilated pupil + headache = CN III compression by PComm aneurysm until proven otherwise → emergency CT angiography

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman on oral contraceptive pills has an established diagnosis of migraine with aura (visual scotoma preceding each headache). She was started on sumatriptan for acute attacks 1 year ago. At today's visit she asks about preventive therapy — she has 6 migraines per month, each lasting 24-36 hours. Which preventive agent is CONTRAINDICATED in this specific patient?

options.A: Topiramate
options.B: Valproic acid
options.C: Propranolol
options.D: Erenumab (CGRP monoclonal antibody)

questions.rationale: Valproic acid (valproate) is a teratogen (Category X): associated with neural tube defects, cognitive impairment, and neonatal hepatotoxicity. It is CONTRAINDICATED in women of reproductive age unless highly effective contraception is in place AND other options have failed, with thorough informed consent. While this patient is on OCP, the FDA REMS program (SHARE) for valproate requires specific documentation. In a 28-year-old with established migraine, valproate should be avoided when alternatives exist. Topiramate (A) is also teratogenic but is classified as 'use with caution and contraception' rather than absolute contraindication in managed settings — still a concern, but the question asks which is CONTRAINDICATED specifically. Propranolol (C) is safe in most women not trying to conceive; avoid in pregnancy but not in women on OCP. Erenumab (D) is a CGRP monoclonal antibody approved for migraine prevention — relatively newer, Category X in pregnancy by convention but commonly used in reproductive-age women with appropriate counseling.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Topiramate is teratogenic but the AAN guidelines specifically list valproate as the agent to avoid in women of childbearing potential without REMS.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Propranolol has a reasonable safety profile for preventive use in women on OCP.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Erenumab is an acceptable preventive option in reproductive-age women with appropriate counseling about stopping before pregnancy.

questions.keyConceptTested: Valproate is contraindicated in women of reproductive age for migraine prophylaxis due to teratogenicity

questions.stem: A 36-year-old man presents with headaches occurring in clusters every spring for the past 4 years. Each attack is described as periorbital, unilateral, excruciating, lasting 45 minutes, occurring 2-3 times per day. During attacks he paces the room and is unable to sit still. He has ipsilateral lacrimation, nasal congestion, and ptosis during attacks. Which acute treatment is MOST effective for this patient?

options.A: Oral sumatriptan 100 mg — first-line triptan for all headache types
options.B: High-flow 100% oxygen 7-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15 minutes; injectable sumatriptan 6 mg SC as alternative
options.C: Prochlorperazine 10 mg IV — effective for all primary headache acute treatment
options.D: Butalbital/acetaminophen/caffeine — appropriate for severe primary headaches

questions.rationale: This is cluster headache: periodic pattern (spring clustering), periorbital unilateral severe pain, autonomic features (lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, Horner's), short duration (15-180 min), restlessness (pathognomonic — patients cannot stay still, unlike migraine). First-line acute treatments are (1) high-flow 100% oxygen 7-15 L/min via non-rebreather for 15 minutes (fastest and safest), and (2) injectable sumatriptan 6 mg SC (fastest medication onset). Oral sumatriptan (A) has too slow an onset (30-60 min) for cluster attacks lasting only 15-180 minutes — absorption too slow relative to attack duration. Prochlorperazine (C) is used IV for migraine in ED settings, not outpatient cluster management. Butalbital compounds (D) are never appropriate for cluster headaches — no evidence, high abuse potential.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Oral sumatriptan is too slow for cluster attacks — injectable SC or nasal spray are preferred.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Prochlorperazine is useful for migraine-associated nausea, not cluster headache.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Butalbital compounds have no role in cluster headache treatment.

questions.keyConceptTested: Cluster headache acute treatment: high-flow O2 and injectable sumatriptan are first-line; oral sumatriptan is too slow

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman reports headaches present nearly every day for the past 4 months. She was previously episodic (2-3 headaches/month). She uses ibuprofen 400 mg or sumatriptan 50 mg on 14-18 days per month. She describes the daily headaches as dull but has superimposed severe attacks 3-4 times per month. Which diagnosis BEST explains her current headache pattern?

options.A: Migraine with aura that has evolved to chronic migraine
options.B: Medication overuse headache (MOH) — the frequent analgesic/triptan use is driving the daily pattern
options.C: New daily persistent headache — requires MRI to rule out secondary cause
options.D: Cluster headache variant — increased frequency suggests a new cluster period

questions.rationale: Medication overuse headache (MOH, previously 'analgesic rebound') develops when patients use acute headache medications ≥10 days/month (triptans, ergotamines) or ≥15 days/month (NSAIDs, acetaminophen) for >3 months. The result is a paradoxical daily or near-daily headache driven by medication overuse. This patient uses treatment agents on 14-18 days/month for 4 months — classic MOH. Treatment requires withdrawal of the offending analgesic (can taper NSAIDs; triptans can be stopped abruptly). The initial withdrawal phase worsens headache for 7-10 days. Concurrently, initiate prophylactic therapy. Chronic migraine (A) is a valid concern but MOH is the primary driver that must be addressed first — removing the MOH often reveals the true underlying pattern. MRI (C) is indicated for new daily headache with red flags but not as initial step in this clearly explained MOH pattern. Cluster (D) has distinctly different features (periorbital, autonomic, restlessness).

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Chronic migraine can coexist but MOH from medication overuse must be addressed first — MOH explains the daily pattern.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: MRI is not the priority when MOH is the most likely diagnosis; investigate further if pattern doesn't improve after medication withdrawal.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Cluster headache is episodic with severe unilateral periorbital pain and autonomic features — does not match this daily dull pattern.

questions.keyConceptTested: Medication overuse headache: analgesic/triptan use ≥10-15 days/month for >3 months drives daily headache pattern; treatment = withdrawal

questions.stem: A 55-year-old immunocompromised (HIV, CD4 120) man presents with 5 days of progressive headache, low-grade fever (37.8°C), and subtle confusion. His wife says he has been 'not himself.' Neck stiffness is present on exam. Which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.A: Order CT head first; if no mass effect or herniation signs → perform lumbar puncture; start empiric antibiotics + antifungal simultaneously
options.B: Administer oral ciprofloxacin empirically for presumed bacterial meningitis and arrange outpatient neurology
options.C: Start empiric IV ceftriaxone before any imaging — do not delay for CT
options.D: Discharge with analgesics and close follow-up — HIV patients frequently have headaches

questions.rationale: In an immunocompromised patient with headache + fever + altered mental status + neck stiffness, the differential includes bacterial meningitis AND cryptococcal meningitis (Cryptococcus neoformans is the most common cause of meningitis in HIV patients with CD4 <200). Management: CT head first to rule out mass lesion or herniation before LP (immunocompromised patients have higher risk of CNS mass lesions). If CT clear → LP with India ink preparation (50% sensitivity for cryptococcal meningitis), cryptococcal antigen (>90% sensitivity), standard bacterial culture, and opening pressure. Start empiric antibiotics AND antifungal (amphotericin B + flucytosine for cryptococcal meningitis; ceftriaxone + dexamethasone for bacterial). Oral ciprofloxacin (B) is wholly inadequate for meningitis. Starting ceftriaxone before CT (C) applies to immunocompetent patients with no papilledema — in immunocompromised, CT first is the guideline to rule out mass lesion. Discharge (D) is inappropriate with meningeal signs.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Correct — this is the appropriate sequence and empiric coverage for immunocompromised meningitis.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Oral antibiotics are not appropriate for bacterial or cryptococcal meningitis.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Immunocompromised patients should have CT before LP due to higher risk of CNS mass lesions causing herniation.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Meningeal signs and altered mental status in an immunocompromised patient is a medical emergency.

questions.keyConceptTested: Immunocompromised meningitis workup: CT head before LP; empiric antibiotics + antifungal; cryptococcal meningitis high in HIV CD4 <200

questions.stem: An 18-year-old male basketball player collapsed during a game. Witnesses report he was running and suddenly fell without any warning prodrome. He regained consciousness within 1 minute and feels fine now. He has no prior syncopal episodes. Vital signs are normal. He has a family history of his uncle dying of sudden cardiac death at age 35. ECG shows voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy. Which is the MOST appropriate management?

options.A: Reassure — vasovagal syncope is most common in young athletes; clear him to return to sport
options.B: Transfer to ED or same-day cardiology referral; restrict all vigorous physical activity until workup complete
options.C: Schedule ECG and echocardiogram in 4-6 weeks — exertional syncope is common in athletes
options.D: Prescribe midodrine for orthostatic hypotension — likely dehydration during exercise

questions.rationale: This young athlete has EXERTIONAL SYNCOPE — the highest-risk syncope scenario. Combined with: no prodrome, family history of sudden cardiac death at 35, and ECG showing LVH — HOCM (hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy) must be excluded urgently. HOCM is the leading cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes in the US. Exertional syncope with LVH on ECG is an absolute contraindication to sports participation until structural cardiac disease is excluded. Same-day cardiology referral + echocardiogram is mandatory. Clearing to return to sport (A) without workup is dangerous and potentially catastrophic. A 4-6 week wait (C) is unacceptably delayed for exertional syncope with a high-risk presentation. Midodrine (D) treats orthostatic hypotension — orthostasis does not cause syncope during exertion without prodrome.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Exertional syncope without prodrome + family history of sudden cardiac death + LVH on ECG = high-risk; cannot be managed as vasovagal.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: 4-6 week delay in evaluating potential HOCM with activity restrictions not enforced is unacceptably dangerous.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Orthostatic hypotension does not cause exertional syncope without prodrome while running.

questions.keyConceptTested: Exertional syncope in young athlete = HOCM until proven otherwise; same-day cardiology referral; no return to sport without clearance

questions.stem: Which of the following ECG findings in a patient presenting with syncope is an ABSOLUTE indication for ED referral?

options.A: Sinus bradycardia at 56 bpm with normal PR and QRS
options.B: QTc of 510 ms
options.C: Left axis deviation
options.D: T-wave inversions in V1-V2 only

questions.rationale: QTc prolongation of 510 ms is a high-risk finding that predisposes to torsades de pointes (polymorphic VT) and sudden cardiac death. A QTc >500 ms is associated with substantially increased risk of lethal arrhythmia and requires immediate ED referral. Normal QTc is ≤450 ms males, ≤470 ms females. Sinus bradycardia at 56 bpm (A) is a normal finding in athletes and does not independently mandate ED referral. Left axis deviation (C) is a common, nonspecific finding not independently high-risk for syncope. T-wave inversions in V1-V2 alone (D) can be normal (especially in women) or represent right heart strain — requires context but not automatically an ED indicator without other findings.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Mild bradycardia in athletes is physiologic — not independently high-risk in syncope.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Left axis deviation is a common, nonspecific finding and not an indicator for ED in syncope.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Isolated T-wave inversions in V1-V2 alone are not high-risk; broader pattern or context would be needed.

questions.keyConceptTested: ECG red flags in syncope: QTc >500 ms = ED referral for torsades de pointes risk

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman passed out in church while standing for a long service on a hot day. Bystanders report she was pale, sweaty, and went limp for about 30 seconds. She woke up quickly and felt normal within a minute. She has no cardiac history, no medications, and her ECG is normal. What is the MOST appropriate management?

options.A: Admit to telemetry for 24-hour cardiac monitoring — all first-time syncope requires observation
options.B: Refer to cardiology urgently — first syncope episode always requires specialist evaluation
options.C: Diagnose vasovagal syncope clinically; educate on counterpressure maneuvers, hydration, and avoidance of prolonged standing in hot environments; close outpatient follow-up
options.D: Order Holter monitor immediately and prescribe midodrine empirically

questions.rationale: This is classic vasovagal syncope: a young healthy woman, trigger (prolonged standing, hot environment), prodrome (pallor, diaphoresis), brief LOC (<60 sec), rapid full recovery, no palpitations, normal ECG, and no high-risk features. Vasovagal syncope can be diagnosed clinically with confidence when the story is classic, physical exam is normal, and ECG is normal. The treatment is patient education (counterpressure maneuvers, increased fluid/salt intake, avoid triggers), not hospitalization or urgent cardiology referral. Admission (A) is not indicated for uncomplicated vasovagal — would add cost without benefit. Cardiology referral (B) is reserved for high-risk or unexplained syncope, not classic vasovagal. Holter monitor (D) is used for recurrent unexplained syncope to detect arrhythmia — not appropriate as first-line for classic vasovagal.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Admission is not warranted for classic, low-risk vasovagal syncope — this is evidence-based and cost-effective.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Cardiology referral is reserved for high-risk or unexplained syncope — classic vasovagal does not require specialist referral.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Holter monitor and midodrine are not first-line for initial uncomplicated vasovagal syncope.

questions.keyConceptTested: Classic vasovagal syncope: clinical diagnosis, patient education on counterpressure + hydration; no hospitalization or urgent cardiology needed

questions.stem: Which clinical feature is MOST predictive of a CARDIAC rather than vasovagal cause of syncope?

options.A: The event was triggered by emotional stress and occurred while the patient was standing
options.B: The patient reports diaphoresis and nausea 30 seconds before losing consciousness
options.C: The episode occurred without warning while the patient was seated, and recovery took 10 minutes
options.D: The patient is 22 years old with a prior history of vasovagal syncope and had one episode after blood draw

questions.rationale: Cardiac syncope is characterized by: (1) NO prodrome or extremely brief prodrome, (2) syncope while seated or supine (not positional/standing-related), (3) prolonged recovery period. A 10-minute recovery (prolonged LOC) and absence of warning while seated are distinctly cardiac features. Vasovagal syncope almost always has a prodromal phase (diaphoresis, nausea, lightheadedness), occurs in upright position, and has very rapid recovery (<1-2 min). Emotional stress trigger and standing position (A) and diaphoresis/nausea prodrome (B) are classic vasovagal features. Prior vasovagal history with a situational trigger (blood draw) (D) is also classic vasovagal.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Emotional trigger + upright posture = vasovagal predictors.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Diaphoresis and nausea prodrome = classic vasovagal features.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Situational trigger (blood draw) + prior vasovagal history = vasovagal syncope.

questions.keyConceptTested: Cardiac syncope: no prodrome, occurs seated/supine, prolonged recovery — contrast with vasovagal: prodrome, upright position, rapid recovery

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man on metoprolol 50 mg BID, lisinopril 10 mg, furosemide 20 mg, and tamsulosin 0.4 mg reports two episodes of lightheadedness and near-fainting when rising from a chair over the past 2 weeks. He feels completely normal when seated. Which is the MOST likely diagnosis and first management step?

options.A: Vasovagal syncope — perform tilt table testing
options.B: Orthostatic hypotension from polypharmacy — check orthostatic vital signs; review and adjust medications
options.C: Arrhythmic syncope — order 30-day cardiac event monitor
options.D: Carotid sinus hypersensitivity — perform carotid sinus massage

questions.rationale: This elderly patient on four medications with known orthostatic properties (beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor, loop diuretic, alpha-blocker for BPH) has classic medication-induced orthostatic hypotension: symptoms only on rising, completely well when seated. Orthostatic vital signs should be measured (supine → standing BP at 1, 2, 3 minutes).
