Management includes medication review: tamsulosin (alpha-blocker) is a common culprit; furosemide contributes volume depletion; metoprolol blunts compensatory tachycardia on standing. Address dehydration, review timing and dosing of antihypertensives. Vasovagal (A) presents with triggers and prodrome in typically younger patients — not a position-specific trigger without prodrome. Arrhythmic syncope (C) is position-independent. Carotid sinus massage (D) is performed for carotid sinus hypersensitivity (syncope with neck turning/collar pressure in older men) — not applicable here.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Vasovagal syncope features are absent — no emotional trigger, no prodrome; the position-specific trigger strongly suggests orthostasis.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Arrhythmic syncope is position-independent; event monitor is not the first step before addressing obvious pharmacologic cause.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Carotid sinus massage is not indicated here; clinical picture is medication-induced orthostasis.

questions.keyConceptTested: Orthostatic hypotension from polypharmacy in elderly: position-specific symptoms, medication review (alpha-blockers, diuretics, beta-blockers)

questions.stem: A patient presents to urgent care after a syncopal episode. Which piece of history is the MOST important in distinguishing seizure from syncope?

options.A: The patient was incontinent during the episode
options.B: The episode lasted approximately 60 seconds
options.C: The patient was confused and disoriented for 20 minutes after regaining consciousness
options.D: The patient's leg jerked during the episode

questions.rationale: Postictal confusion lasting minutes to an hour is the most discriminating feature of seizure versus syncope. After a syncopal episode, recovery is rapid and complete (seconds to 1-2 minutes with essentially full orientation). A 20-minute period of confusion and disorientation strongly suggests seizure with postictal state. Incontinence (A) can occur in both seizure and syncope — it is not discriminating. Duration of 60 seconds (B) can occur in both conditions. Brief myoclonic jerks (D) can occur in syncope (anoxic seizure — brief oxygen deprivation causes muscle twitches) and should NOT be over-interpreted as seizure — the duration matters: >30-60 seconds of rhythmic clonic movements = seizure; brief (<10 sec) myoclonic jerks after collapse = can be anoxic syncope.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Incontinence occurs in both syncope and seizure — not discriminating.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Duration alone does not distinguish seizure from syncope.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Brief myoclonic jerks during syncope (anoxic myoclonus) are common and should not be misinterpreted as epileptic seizure.

questions.keyConceptTested: Seizure vs syncope: prolonged postictal confusion is the most discriminating feature — rapid full recovery favors syncope

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man presents to urgent care with BP 196/118 and severe headache for 2 hours. He stopped his lisinopril 3 weeks ago because he ran out. He is alert and oriented. Fundoscopic exam is attempted but cannot be completed. There is no focal neurological deficit, no chest pain, no dyspnea. Which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.A: Administer sublingual nifedipine to rapidly lower BP
options.B: Call 911 for emergency transport — all BP >180/120 requires hospital admission
options.C: Have the patient rest for 20-30 minutes; re-check BP; resume lisinopril; arrange 24-48 hour follow-up if repeat BP is improved and exam remains normal
options.D: Start IV labetalol in urgent care to rapidly reduce MAP by 30% in 1 hour

questions.rationale: This patient meets criteria for HYPERTENSIVE URGENCY: BP ≥180/120 WITHOUT evidence of acute end-organ damage. He is alert, neurologically intact, has no chest pain, no dyspnea, and no fundoscopic changes identified. The headache likely reflects severely elevated BP without encephalopathy (no confusion, no vomiting, no visual changes, no focal deficit). Appropriate management of urgency: rest in a quiet room for 20-30 minutes (white coat effect can account for a significant portion of the elevation), restart/resume his antihypertensive (lisinopril), and arrange close outpatient follow-up within 24-48 hours. Do NOT administer sublingual nifedipine (A) — this is specifically dangerous and is associated with precipitating MI and stroke by causing uncontrolled rapid BP drop. Hospital admission (B) is not required for urgency without end-organ damage. IV labetalol (D) is for hypertensive emergency requiring IV management — not outpatient urgency.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Sublingual nifedipine is dangerous — rapid uncontrolled BP drop can precipitate stroke and MI; it is contraindicated.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Hypertensive urgency (without end-organ damage) does not mandate hospital admission — oral agents and close follow-up are appropriate.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: IV antihypertensives are for hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage — not urgency in an alert, neurologically intact patient.

questions.keyConceptTested: Hypertensive urgency: no end-organ damage → rest + oral agents + 24-48h follow-up; sublingual nifedipine is CONTRAINDICATED

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with known hypertension presents with BP 210/125, sudden onset severe ripping chest pain radiating to the back, and a 20 mmHg BP difference between right (210/125) and left (190/115) arms. Which diagnosis is MOST likely, and what is the CORRECT initial management?

options.A: Hypertensive urgency — administer oral amlodipine 10 mg and recheck in 2 hours
options.B: Acute MI — give sublingual nitroglycerin and aspirin; call 911
options.C: Aortic dissection — call 911 IMMEDIATELY; target SBP <120 mmHg with IV beta-blocker + vasodilator to reduce shear force
options.D: Hypertensive emergency with cardiac involvement — start oral labetalol 100 mg

questions.rationale: The combination of: ripping/tearing chest pain radiating to the back, significantly elevated BP, and inter-arm BP differential (>20 mmHg systolic) is the classic presentation of aortic dissection — a vascular catastrophe requiring immediate surgical evaluation. Call 911. Management targets: reduce SBP to <120 mmHg AND heart rate <60 bpm to reduce arterial wall shear force. IV beta-blocker (esmolol, labetalol) is first-line (controls both BP and HR); add sodium nitroprusside or nicardipine as vasodilator only AFTER beta-blockade (pure vasodilators can cause reflex tachycardia that worsens shear force). Oral medications (A, D) are completely inadequate for this vascular emergency. Aspirin and nitroglycerin (B) could worsen outcomes: aspirin's antiplatelet effects are harmful in dissection; nitroglycerin without HR control is dangerous.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Aortic dissection is a vascular emergency requiring immediate IV management and surgical evaluation — oral medications are contraindicated.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: While aortic dissection can mimic MI, aspirin (antiplatelet) is harmful if dissection has occurred; nitroglycerin without HR control worsens dissection.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Oral labetalol is not appropriate for aortic dissection — IV medication and emergent imaging are required.

questions.keyConceptTested: Aortic dissection: ripping pain + inter-arm BP differential → 911; IV beta-blocker first (reduces HR AND BP); target SBP <120

questions.stem: A 31-year-old woman at 36 weeks gestation presents with BP 168/110, severe headache, and right upper quadrant pain. Urine protein is 3+ on dipstick. Labs show elevated AST/ALT and thrombocytopenia (platelets 78,000). Which condition is MOST likely, and what is the immediate priority?

options.A: Hypertensive urgency in pregnancy — administer oral labetalol and discharge
options.B: Severe preeclampsia with HELLP syndrome — call obstetrics/911 immediately; magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis; IV antihypertensive
options.C: Gestational hypertension — recheck BP in 1 week; no intervention needed
options.D: Chronic hypertension exacerbation — adjust methyldopa dose

questions.rationale: This patient has HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) complicating severe preeclampsia: BP ≥160/110, severe headache, RUQ pain (liver capsule distension), elevated transaminases, and thrombocytopenia. This is an obstetric emergency with risk of eclampsia (seizure), liver rupture, DIC, and placental abruption. Immediate actions: (1) Call obstetrics and 911, (2) magnesium sulfate IV for seizure prophylaxis (also treats/prevents eclampsia), (3) IV antihypertensive (labetalol or hydralazine) to reduce BP, (4) stabilize for delivery — delivery is the definitive treatment. Oral labetalol discharge (A) is dangerously inadequate for BP ≥160/110 with HELLP. 'Gestational hypertension' (C) is below the severity threshold — BP ≥160/110 + HELLP = severe preeclampsia. Adjusting methyldopa (D) is completely insufficient.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: BP ≥160/110 + HELLP criteria is not urgency — this is a maternal emergency requiring immediate hospitalization.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: HELLP syndrome with BP ≥160/110 and proteinuria is not gestational hypertension — it is severe preeclampsia with life-threatening complications.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Oral medication adjustment is inadequate for HELLP syndrome — IV treatment and delivery are required.

questions.keyConceptTested: HELLP syndrome: preeclampsia with hemolysis, elevated LFTs, thrombocytopenia = obstetric emergency; MgSO4, IV antihypertensive, delivery

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man has blood pressure measured at 184/110 and 188/112 on two occasions in the office today. He has no symptoms. Review of his medications reveals he takes amlodipine 10 mg, lisinopril 40 mg, and hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg — all at maximum doses. He is compliant with medications. Spironolactone has not been tried. What is the MOST appropriate next action?

options.A: Diagnose resistant hypertension; add spironolactone 25-50 mg daily; workup for secondary hypertension causes
options.B: Refer to ED for hypertensive emergency management
options.C: Double the dose of lisinopril to 80 mg daily
options.D: Discharge and advise low-sodium diet as the only needed adjustment

questions.rationale: Resistant hypertension is defined as BP above goal despite ≥3 antihypertensive agents at maximum tolerated doses including a diuretic (with confirmed adherence). This patient meets all criteria: on 3 agents at max dose, compliant, still uncontrolled. Next step: (1) add spironolactone 25-50 mg/day — primary hyperaldosteronism is highly prevalent in resistant HTN and spironolactone is the most effective add-on regardless of aldosterone status, (2) workup for secondary causes (serum aldosterone/plasma renin activity, renal ultrasound for RAS, TSH, sleep study for OSA). ED referral (B) is not indicated — this is not a hypertensive emergency (no end-organ damage; the BP elevation is chronic, not acute). Lisinopril cannot be doubled to 80 mg (C) — 40 mg is the maximum recommended dose. Diet alone (D) is insufficient for resistant hypertension.

incorrectOptionExplanations.B: No end-organ damage and no acute severity — not a hypertensive emergency; resistant HTN management in outpatient setting.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: 40 mg is the maximum recommended dose of lisinopril; doubling is not evidence-based.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Dietary advice alone is insufficient when medications at max doses have failed to control BP.

questions.keyConceptTested: Resistant hypertension: add spironolactone as 4th agent; workup for secondary causes including primary hyperaldosteronism

questions.stem: A 77-year-old woman on amlodipine 10 mg and losartan 100 mg has SBP consistently 148-156 mmHg over the past 3 months. DBP is 68-72 mmHg. She reports dizziness on standing. Orthostatic vitals show a drop from SBP 150 to 118 when standing for 2 minutes. Which is the MOST appropriate management?

options.A: Add a third antihypertensive to achieve SBP <130
options.B: Reduce antihypertensive therapy; address orthostatic hypotension; target SBP 130-140 for her age
options.C: Switch to a beta-blocker — more effective for isolated systolic hypertension
options.D: Refer to cardiology for implantable device to stabilize blood pressure

questions.rationale: This older woman has two problems: (1) incompletely controlled isolated systolic HTN (SBP 148-156), and (2) significant orthostatic hypotension (SBP drop of >30 mmHg on standing) — a fall risk. In frail elderly patients, the risk of orthostatic hypotension causing falls often outweighs the benefit of tighter systolic control. Current guidelines recommend SBP target 130-140 in elderly patients without frailty; in frail elderly with symptomatic orthostasis, a less stringent target (140-150) may be appropriate. Reducing one antihypertensive (consider reducing amlodipine or losartan dose) should be the first step to address the orthostatic hypotension. Adding a third agent (A) when orthostasis is already causing symptoms would worsen fall risk significantly. Beta-blockers (C) are not preferred for isolated systolic HTN in the elderly. Cardiology implantable device (D) has no role in HTN management.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Adding more antihypertensives when symptomatic orthostasis already exists will worsen fall risk — inappropriate.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Beta-blockers are not the preferred class for isolated systolic HTN in the elderly and would worsen orthostasis.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: No such device exists for hypertension management in this context.

questions.keyConceptTested: Elderly with orthostatic hypotension and partially controlled systolic HTN: reduce therapy to address fall risk; prioritize orthostasis over tight BP control

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with hypertension controlled on lisinopril presents with BP 188/116 and new onset confusion, headache, and blurry vision. You perform fundoscopy and observe bilateral papilledema with flame hemorrhages. Creatinine has risen from 1.1 to 2.3 mg/dL since last month. What is the CORRECT classification and next step?

options.A: Hypertensive urgency — adjust oral medications and recheck in 48 hours
options.B: Hypertensive emergency with evidence of end-organ damage (brain, kidney, eye) — transfer to ED immediately
options.C: Chronic hypertensive nephropathy — schedule nephrology referral within 2 weeks
options.D: White coat hypertension — order ambulatory blood pressure monitoring

questions.rationale: This patient has HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY: BP ≥180/120 WITH multiple simultaneous end-organ damage manifestations: hypertensive encephalopathy (confusion + headache), hypertensive retinopathy (papilledema + flame hemorrhages), and hypertensive nephropathy (acute creatinine doubling). This is a medical emergency requiring immediate ED transfer for IV antihypertensive therapy with controlled reduction (reduce MAP by 10-15% in first hour, 15-25% over next 23 hours — NOT a rapid normalization). Visual changes with papilledema and acute kidney injury clearly exclude the diagnosis of urgency. Nephrology referral in 2 weeks (C) is dangerously delayed for acute creatinine doubling with hypertensive crisis. Ambulatory BP monitoring (D) is used to diagnose white coat hypertension — inappropriate when the patient has active end-organ damage.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: End-organ damage (confusion, papilledema, acute kidney injury) converts this from urgency to emergency — not manageable with oral agents outpatient.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Acute creatinine doubling with BP crisis requires emergency management, not routine nephrology referral.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Active end-organ damage rules out white coat hypertension; ABPM is irrelevant in this emergency.

questions.keyConceptTested: Hypertensive emergency: BP ≥180/120 + end-organ damage (encephalopathy, papilledema, AKI) → immediate ED transfer

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man presents with a 2-month history of progressive difficulty swallowing solids (now also liquids), 18-pound unintentional weight loss, and no heartburn. He smokes 1 PPD and drinks 2 glasses of wine nightly. Which combination of ALARM symptoms and workup is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Diagnose GERD empirically; start omeprazole 20 mg daily and reassess in 8 weeks
options.B: Prescribe soft diet and refer to speech therapy for dysphagia evaluation
options.C: Refer urgently for upper endoscopy (EGD) within 2 weeks — progressive dysphagia + weight loss meets ALARM criteria for upper GI malignancy
options.D: Order a barium swallow — safest initial imaging for dysphagia

questions.rationale: This patient has multiple ALARM (red flag) features for esophageal or gastric malignancy: progressive dysphagia (from solids to liquids = obstructive lesion advancing), significant weight loss (18 lbs), and risk factors (smoking, alcohol, male, age >50). ALARM criteria mandate upper endoscopy (EGD) without delay — empiric acid suppression without endoscopy in this setting risks delaying a cancer diagnosis. The NICE NG12 guideline recommends urgent EGD within 2 weeks for patients with dysphagia or dyspepsia with red flags. Starting omeprazole without EGD (A) would delay diagnosis — acceptable only for uncomplicated dyspepsia without red flags. Speech therapy (B) is appropriate for neurological dysphagia (oropharyngeal, stroke-related) — not for obstructive structural lesion. Barium swallow (D) can identify esophageal lesions but does not allow biopsy — EGD is required for definitive diagnosis.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: ALARM symptoms prohibit empiric acid suppression without endoscopy — dysphagia + weight loss = urgent workup.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Speech therapy is for neurological/oropharyngeal dysphagia, not progressive esophageal obstruction.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Barium swallow cannot provide tissue diagnosis — EGD with biopsy is required for suspected esophageal malignancy.

questions.keyConceptTested: ALARM criteria: progressive dysphagia + weight loss + risk factors = urgent EGD within 2 weeks; no empiric PPI without endoscopy

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man presents with 3 episodes of gross (visible) hematuria over 2 months, each painless and self-resolving. He is a former smoker. Physical exam including genital exam is unremarkable. Urinalysis today shows no blood (done 2 weeks after last episode). What is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.A: Reassure — urinalysis today is negative; he does not have hematuria
options.B: Diagnose benign prostatic hyperplasia — common in his age group and can cause hematuria
options.C: Refer to urology for cystoscopy and CT urogram — visible hematuria in an adult ≥35 requires workup regardless of current UA result
options.D: Prescribe tamsulosin for BPH and reassess in 3 months

questions.rationale: Gross (visible) hematuria in an adult ≥35 is an ABSOLUTE indication for urology referral and cystoscopy + CT urogram, REGARDLESS of whether subsequent urinalysis is negative. Bladder cancer and renal cell carcinoma commonly present with intermittent painless hematuria. A negative UA when the patient is not currently bleeding does not exclude a lesion — the episodes can be episodic. The AUA recommends cystoscopy for all adults ≥35 with gross hematuria (even single episode) or microscopic hematuria without explanation. BPH can cause hematuria but is a diagnosis of exclusion — urological malignancy must be ruled out first. Tamsulosin for BPH (D) without ruling out malignancy first is inappropriate.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Three prior episodes of gross hematuria constitute a clinical history that requires workup — a negative UA at a different time does not negate this.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: BPH is a diagnosis of exclusion — bladder cancer and RCC must be excluded first in a male smoker >60 with gross hematuria.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: BPH treatment without ruling out malignancy in a symptomatic gross hematuria case is inappropriate — workup first.

questions.keyConceptTested: Gross hematuria in adult ≥35: urology referral + cystoscopy + CT urogram mandatory regardless of current UA — intermittent bleeding does not negate workup need

questions.stem: A 71-year-old woman presents with 3 months of generalized fatigue, 12-pound weight loss, and night sweats requiring change of nightclothes. She has no fever. On exam, you note a 2 cm firm, non-tender left supraclavicular lymph node. Which statement is MOST accurate?

options.A: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy in an elderly patient is commonly reactive; observe for 8 weeks before workup
options.B: A supraclavicular lymph node + B-symptoms (weight loss, night sweats, fever) suggests lymphoma or other malignancy requiring urgent workup
options.C: Order a course of antibiotics first — infectious lymphadenopathy must be excluded before malignancy
options.D: Reassure — night sweats are common in postmenopausal women and the weight loss is modest

questions.rationale: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (Virchow's node on the left) is ALWAYS concerning for malignancy — it represents drainage from the thoracic and abdominal organs and is highly associated with lymphoma, GI, lung, and breast malignancy. Combined with B-symptoms (weight loss >10%, drenching night sweats, ± fever) — this pattern demands urgent cancer evaluation. Workup: CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, LDH (elevated in lymphoma), uric acid, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, HIV, and hematology/oncology referral. 'Observe for 8 weeks' (A) is wrong — supraclavicular nodes are never watched; they are worked up urgently. Empiric antibiotics (C) delay diagnosis — there is no infectious context here. Normalizing night sweats as menopausal (D) misses the B-symptom triad context and concurrent supraclavicular adenopathy.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy is NEVER observed without workup — it is high-risk by definition.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Antibiotics are not appropriate without evidence of infection; empiric antibiotics delay malignancy diagnosis.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Night sweats + weight loss + supraclavicular node = B-symptoms suggesting malignancy; cannot be attributed to menopause.

questions.keyConceptTested: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy + B-symptoms = urgent malignancy workup; Virchow's node never observed without evaluation

questions.stem: A 49-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a palpable right breast mass she noticed 3 weeks ago. It is 2 cm, firm, non-tender, and non-mobile on exam. She has no nipple discharge, no skin changes. Her last mammogram 2 years ago was normal. Which is the MOST appropriate next step?

options.A: Repeat mammogram in 1 year — prior normal mammogram reduces cancer risk
options.B: Prescribe evening primrose oil and reassure — breast pain and masses are common in postmenopausal women
options.C: Order diagnostic mammogram + breast ultrasound; refer to surgery or breast specialist for evaluation
options.D: Observe for 6 weeks — if the mass persists, then order imaging

questions.rationale: A palpable breast mass in a postmenopausal woman requires imaging and tissue diagnosis — a prior normal mammogram does NOT eliminate cancer risk and is not reassuring in the face of a NEW palpable abnormality. Diagnostic mammogram (not screening) + breast ultrasound should be ordered urgently. Refer to breast surgery or breast specialist for evaluation, which may include core needle biopsy based on imaging findings. In postmenopausal women, any new palpable mass has significantly higher malignancy risk than in premenopausal women. A 6-week observation period (D) is inappropriate for a new palpable breast mass in a postmenopausal woman — delayed diagnosis of breast cancer worsens prognosis. Options A and B are dangerously inadequate.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: A prior normal mammogram does not negate the clinical significance of a new palpable mass — diagnostic imaging is required.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Evening primrose oil and reassurance are not appropriate for a new palpable breast mass in a postmenopausal woman.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: 6-week observation is not appropriate for a firm, non-mobile mass in a postmenopausal patient — immediate imaging is required.

questions.keyConceptTested: Palpable breast mass in postmenopausal woman: diagnostic mammogram + ultrasound + specialist referral; prior normal mammogram does not reassure

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with a 40 pack-year smoking history presents for her annual visit. She has no respiratory symptoms and is in good health. She quit smoking 3 years ago. She asks about lung cancer screening. Which recommendation is MOST consistent with current USPSTF guidelines?

options.A: No screening recommended — she quit 3 years ago and is no longer high-risk
options.B: Annual low-dose CT (LDCT) scan of the chest — she meets criteria: age 50-80, 20+ pack-year history, current smoker OR quit within the past 15 years
options.C: Annual chest X-ray — adequate for lung cancer screening in former smokers
options.D: Sputum cytology every 2 years — appropriate screening in symptomatic smokers

questions.rationale: USPSTF 2021 recommendation: annual low-dose CT (LDCT) lung cancer screening for adults aged 50-80 who have a 20 pack-year smoking history AND currently smoke OR quit within the past 15 years. This patient: age 65, 40 pack-year history, quit 3 years ago (within 15 years) — she meets ALL criteria. Screening can stop when: age >80, >15 years since quitting, or health problems limiting treatment or life expectancy. Screening stops at 15 years post-cessation (A is incorrect — 3 years does not disqualify). Chest X-ray (C) has NO proven mortality benefit for lung cancer screening and is NOT recommended. Sputum cytology (D) is not recommended — insufficient sensitivity and not a USPSTF-endorsed strategy.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: She quit only 3 years ago — screening continues for 15 years after cessation; she remains a screening candidate.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Chest X-ray has no mortality benefit for lung cancer screening — LDCT is the only recommended modality.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Sputum cytology is not a recommended screening strategy for lung cancer.

questions.keyConceptTested: USPSTF lung cancer screening: LDCT annually, age 50-80, ≥20 pack-years, current or quit within 15 years — chest X-ray NOT recommended

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man who has sex with men (MSM) presents requesting PrEP. He reports condomless anal sex with 2-3 partners per month. He had a gonorrhea diagnosis 4 months ago. His most recent HIV test was 6 months ago (negative). Which is the MOST appropriate first step before prescribing PrEP?

options.A: Prescribe Truvada based on the negative HIV test from 6 months ago
options.B: Order a 4th-generation HIV antigen/antibody test (or HIV RNA); withhold PrEP until result is confirmed negative within the past 7 days
options.C: Start PrEP and simultaneously treat presumed gonorrhea — the prior infection is a contraindication
options.D: Refer to infectious disease before prescribing PrEP as a first-time prescriber

questions.rationale: Before prescribing PrEP, a RECENT negative HIV test (within 7 days of initiation) is mandatory. An HIV test from 6 months ago is not adequate — the patient may have acquired HIV in the interim (he has risk behaviors and had bacterial STI 4 months ago, suggesting ongoing exposure). Starting PrEP in an HIV-positive individual risks developing drug resistance to tenofovir and emtricitabine, the backbones of most ART regimens. The gold standard pre-PrEP HIV test: 4th-generation HIV-1/2 antigen/antibody assay. Prescribing based on a 6-month-old test (A) is medically inappropriate. Prior gonorrhea (C) is not a contraindication — in fact it is an indicator of higher risk that strengthens PrEP eligibility. Infectious disease referral (D) is not required for PrEP initiation — NPs are authorized and expected to prescribe PrEP independently.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: A 6-month-old HIV test is inadequate — must confirm HIV-negative status within 7 days of starting PrEP.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Prior gonorrhea is an eligibility indicator for PrEP, not a contraindication. Prior STI increases urgency for PrEP.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: NPs are fully qualified to prescribe PrEP without specialist referral — this is core NP competency.

questions.keyConceptTested: PrEP initiation: requires negative HIV test within 7 days — 6-month-old test is insufficient; prior STI strengthens eligibility, not a contraindication

questions.stem: A 34-year-old transgender woman (assigned male at birth, currently on estrogen therapy) requests PrEP. She has receptive anal sex with multiple cisgender male partners and reports no current condom use. Which PrEP regimen is MOST appropriate, and which monitoring schedule is REQUIRED?

options.A: TAF/FTC (Descovy) — approved for transgender women; HIV test annually
options.B: TDF/FTC (Truvada) OR TAF/FTC (Descovy) — both approved for transgender women and MSM; HIV test every 3 months; STI screening every 3-6 months; renal function at baseline and q6 months for Truvada
options.C: TAF/FTC only — Truvada is contraindicated with estrogen due to drug interactions
options.D: Injectable cabotegravir only — oral PrEP is not approved for transgender women

questions.rationale: Both TDF/FTC (Truvada) and TAF/FTC (Descovy) are FDA-approved for PrEP in transgender women for receptive anal sex. There are NO clinically significant drug-drug interactions between either TDF/FTC or TAF/FTC and gender-affirming estrogen therapy. Monitoring requirements are the same for any PrEP regimen: HIV test every 3 months (mandatory), STI screening every 3-6 months (anatomic site-specific NAAT), and for Truvada specifically — renal function (CrCl) every 6 months due to TDF's tubular toxicity. TAF has better renal and bone safety. Injectable cabotegravir (C) is also approved and an excellent option for transgender women (HPTN 083 trial included them) but is not the ONLY option — oral PrEP is fully appropriate. Truvada is not contraindicated with estrogen (C — incorrect statement).

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: HIV test annually is wrong — mandatory HIV testing every 3 months for all PrEP users.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: There are no significant drug interactions between Truvada and estrogen therapy — this statement is incorrect.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Oral PrEP is approved and appropriate for transgender women — injectable is an option, not the only option.

questions.keyConceptTested: PrEP for transgender women: both Truvada and Descovy approved; no interaction with estrogen; HIV test every 3 months mandatory

questions.stem: A patient has been on TDF/FTC (Truvada) PrEP for 8 months. At today's 3-month monitoring visit, his HIV antigen/antibody test returns REACTIVE. HIV RNA PCR is sent and returns at 22,000 copies/mL. What is the MOST appropriate immediate next step?

options.A: Continue Truvada — 22,000 copies/mL is within the expected PrEP drug-level fluctuation range
options.B: Stop Truvada immediately; urgent referral to HIV specialist within days for drug resistance testing and ART initiation; do not start new antiretrovirals until resistance profile known
options.C: Switch to TAF/FTC (Descovy) — better efficacy in breakthrough infection
options.D: Add a third antiretroviral (dolutegravir) to the current TDF/FTC to create a full treatment regimen

questions.rationale: HIV seroconversion while on PrEP represents a PrEP failure and requires immediate and careful management. The critical danger: TDF/FTC alone is NOT a complete ART regimen — it is a 2-drug backbone. If TDF/FTC is continued as the only antivirals when HIV-positive, the virus will develop resistance to tenofovir and emtricitabine (M184V and K65R mutations), severely limiting future ART options. Actions: (1) STOP Truvada immediately, (2) send resistance testing (genotype) on the CURRENT sample before starting any ART — viral fitness is higher when treatment naive, (3) urgent HIV specialist referral within days, (4) do NOT add empiric dolutegravir without knowing the resistance profile — timing of ART initiation should be guided by specialist. Switching to TAF/FTC (C) is equally inappropriate — it provides the same 2-drug backbone inadequate for HIV treatment. Adding dolutegravir (D) without resistance testing is premature.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: 22,000 copies/mL confirms active HIV infection, not drug-level fluctuation — PrEP failure is occurring.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: TAF/FTC has the same active drugs as Truvada — switching provides no benefit; still an incomplete HIV treatment regimen.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Adding antiretrovirals without knowing the resistance profile can select for resistance — specialist guidance is required first.

questions.keyConceptTested: HIV seroconversion on PrEP: stop Truvada immediately → resistance testing before new ART → urgent HIV specialist referral

questions.stem: A 26-year-old MSM on TDF/FTC PrEP reports that he sometimes misses doses when he is traveling for work. He asks about the minimum number of days he needs to take Truvada before anal sex to be protected. What is the CORRECT answer?

options.A: 1 day — TDF/FTC protects immediately after the first dose
options.B: 3 days — TDF/FTC reaches protective levels in rectal tissue within 72 hours
options.C: 7 days — consistent daily dosing for at least 7 days is required for full protection with rectal exposure in MSM
options.D: 21 days — the protection timeline is the same for all exposure types

questions.rationale: The time to protection varies by exposure type for TDF/FTC: Rectal exposure (insertive or receptive anal sex): 7 days of consistent daily dosing required for full protection. Vaginal exposure: 21 days. Injection drug use: 21 days. This difference reflects tissue concentration kinetics — rectal tissue achieves protective drug levels faster than cervicovaginal tissue. For MSM with rectal exposure, 7 days is the minimum. Missing doses below this threshold may not provide adequate protection. 1 day (A) is incorrect — TDF needs time to accumulate in rectal tissue. 3 days (B) is insufficient — 7 days is the established minimum for rectal. 21 days (D) applies to vaginal and injection exposure, not rectal.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: TDF/FTC does not provide immediate protection — it requires time to reach protective tissue concentrations.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: 3 days is insufficient for rectal protection — 7 days is required.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: 21 days applies to vaginal tissue and injection exposure, not rectal exposure in MSM.

questions.keyConceptTested: PrEP protection timeline: rectal exposure = 7 days; vaginal/IDU exposure = 21 days of consistent dosing

questions.stem: A 32-year-old cisgender woman with CrCl 52 mL/min requests PrEP for protection during vaginal sex with her HIV-positive partner (who is on ART but has an unknown viral load). Which PrEP regimen is MOST appropriate for this patient?

options.A: TAF/FTC (Descovy) — superior for vaginal exposure and better renal safety than Truvada
options.B: TDF/FTC (Truvada) — approved for all eligible individuals including cisgender women; renal monitoring required given CrCl 52
options.C: Injectable cabotegravir (Apretude) — preferred for all serodiscordant couples
options.D: No PrEP indicated — serodiscordant couple where partner is on ART has near-zero risk

questions.rationale: For cisgender women, TDF/FTC (Truvada) is the only FDA-approved oral PrEP — TAF/FTC (Descovy) is NOT approved for cisgender women or transgender men for vaginal exposure (not studied in clinical trials for vaginal exposure). With CrCl 52, Truvada can still be prescribed but requires careful renal monitoring (eGFR every 6 months; consider switching to injectable cabotegravir if renal function declines further). Descovy (A) is NOT approved for vaginal exposure — this is a critical prescribing distinction. Injectable cabotegravir (C) is approved for cisgender women (HPTN 084 trial) and is excellent for this patient, but it requires clinic visits every 2 months and is not universally preferred in all serodiscordant couples. Option D is incorrect — while U=U (Undetectable = Untransmittable) is real when viral load is confirmed undetectable, the partner's viral load is UNKNOWN here. PrEP is appropriate until viral suppression is confirmed and maintained.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Descovy is NOT FDA-approved for vaginal exposure in cisgender women or transgender men — this is a critical regulatory distinction.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Injectable cabotegravir is an option but not universally preferred; Truvada is appropriate for this patient.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: U=U applies when viral load is confirmed undetectable — 'unknown viral load' does not constitute adequate protection.

questions.keyConceptTested: Descovy NOT approved for vaginal exposure in cisgender women — Truvada or injectable cabotegravir are the approved options

questions.stem: A 24-year-old MSM patient on TDF/FTC PrEP mentions he has been using HIV PrEP for 10 months and has never been tested for anal cancer or received an anal Pap. He has had multiple anal sexual partners. What is the MOST evidence-informed approach regarding anal cancer screening?

options.A: Anal Pap smear is not recommended unless the patient is HIV-positive
options.B: Anal Pap is not validated — no routine screening is recommended for any MSM population
options.C: Offer anal Pap cytology — MSM (especially HIV-positive MSM) have significantly elevated anal HPV exposure; the ACS and IDSA recommend consideration for HIV-positive MSM; extend this discussion to HIV-negative MSM with multiple partners as clinical context
options.D: Recommend anal Pap annually for ALL adults regardless of sexual orientation

questions.rationale: Anal cancer screening via anal Pap cytology is a nuanced area: the strongest evidence and most widely endorsed recommendations apply to HIV-positive MSM (prevalence of anal HSIL and cancer dramatically elevated). For HIV-negative MSM with multiple partners, evidence is growing but guidelines vary by organization. IDSA recommends anal Pap for HIV-positive MSM; the New York State Department of Health and many LGBTQ+ health guidelines extend this to high-risk HIV-negative MSM. Inclusive, culturally competent care means discussing this with the patient and offering it as a clinical option with the caveat that formal universal screening guidelines for HIV-negative MSM are still evolving. Option A is incorrect — it is too restrictive; HIV-negative MSM with extensive HPV exposure benefit from discussion. Option B incorrectly states no screening is recommended. Option D is too broad — anal Pap is not recommended universally for all adults.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Too restrictive — anal HPV exposure is high in HIV-negative MSM with multiple partners; screening discussion is appropriate.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Guidelines do recommend anal Pap for HIV-positive MSM — stating no screening is recommended is incorrect.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Anal Pap is not universally recommended for all adults — it targets high-risk populations.

questions.keyConceptTested: Anal cancer screening in MSM: strongly recommended for HIV-positive MSM; offer discussion and screening in HIV-negative MSM with multiple partners

questions.stem: A 30-year-old patient who identifies as bisexual expresses discomfort discussing their sexual history with clinic staff. When you meet them, they disclose they have been afraid to ask for PrEP because they feared judgment. What is the MOST appropriate approach as an NP to create an inclusive, effective clinical encounter?

options.A: Reassure the patient they can trust you; take a sexual history using anatomical and behavioral language (not assumed identities); use the patient's stated pronouns and language
options.B: Have the patient complete a standardized heterosexual-oriented sexual history form first, then adapt questions if needed
options.C: Ask a colleague to conduct the sexual history because the patient expressed discomfort
options.D: Focus only on the PrEP prescription; a full sexual history is not necessary if the patient is requesting PrEP

questions.rationale: Creating an affirming clinical environment is both an ethical obligation and a clinical requirement for LGBTQ+ patients, who face documented higher rates of healthcare avoidance, stigma, and inadequate care. The correct approach: acknowledge the patient's courage in seeking care, explicitly validate their identity, use anatomical and behavioral language rather than assumed identity-based terminology (ask what types of sex they have and with whom, not 'are you gay?'), use the patient's preferred pronouns and language, and explain that this information guides clinical care without judgment. A full, inclusive sexual history is essential — PrEP eligibility and regimen selection depend on specific anatomical exposures and partner types. Standardized heterosexual-oriented forms (B) create barriers and invalidate LGBTQ+ patients. Delegating to a colleague (C) avoids the clinical responsibility. Skipping the sexual history (D) is medically incorrect — exposure type determines which PrEP regimen is appropriate.

incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Heterosexual-oriented forms are exclusionary and inadequate for LGBTQ+ patients; they communicate implicit bias.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Delegating avoids the NP's responsibility; it may also feel stigmatizing to the patient.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: The sexual history is clinically necessary for PrEP eligibility confirmation and regimen selection — it cannot be skipped.

questions.keyConceptTested: Inclusive LGBTQ+ clinical encounter: anatomical/behavioral language, pronouns, validation — sexual history required for appropriate PrEP regimen selection

questions.stem: A 79-year-old woman comes in after her second fall in 3 months. She has hypertension, osteoarthritis, and anxiety. Current medications include amlodipine 10 mg, losartan 50 mg, zolpidem 10 mg nightly, lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN, and ibuprofen 400 mg PRN. She reports difficulty falling asleep. The Timed Up and Go test takes 16 seconds. Which medication change is MOST likely to reduce her fall risk?

options.A: Switch ibuprofen to celecoxib — less GI toxicity
options.B: Discontinue zolpidem and lorazepam; initiate CBT-I and consider low-dose doxepin 3-6 mg if pharmacotherapy needed for sleep
options.C: Add mirtazapine for anxiety and insomnia — dual benefit
options.D: Add gabapentin 100 mg nightly — effective for sleep and anxiety in older adults

questions.rationale: Zolpidem (Z-drug) and lorazepam (benzodiazepine) are BOTH Beers Criteria medications and major fall risk contributors through sedation, psychomotor impairment, and next-morning cognitive slowing. TUG of 16 seconds (≥12 sec = high fall risk) confirms objectively elevated fall risk. The highest priority deprescribing target is the combination of a Z-drug + benzodiazepine. CBT-I is the first-line treatment for insomnia — more effective than pharmacotherapy for chronic insomnia in older adults. If pharmacotherapy is needed, low-dose doxepin 3-6 mg (FDA-approved for sleep maintenance insomnia in older adults) has minimal anticholinergic activity at this dose. Switching to celecoxib (A) is a reasonable GI protection step but doesn't address the fall risk. Mirtazapine (C) is sedating and has anticholinergic and orthostatic properties — worsens fall risk. Gabapentin (D) is a Beers Criteria medication for older adults — increases sedation and fall risk.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Celecoxib reduces GI risk but doesn't address the major fall risk from sedating medications.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Mirtazapine is sedating, has anticholinergic effects, and causes orthostasis — increases, not decreases, fall risk.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Gabapentin is listed in Beers Criteria for older adults due to dizziness and fall risk — contraproductive here.

questions.keyConceptTested: Benzodiazepines and Z-drugs are top Beers Criteria fall-risk medications in elderly — deprescribe first; CBT-I is first-line for insomnia

questions.stem: A 83-year-old man scores 2/5 on the Fried Frailty Criteria (exhaustion and slow gait speed). His daughter asks you about the prognosis implications of this score. Which statement is MOST accurate?

options.A: A score of 2 means he is 'pre-frail' — he has elevated risk but is not frail; targeted exercise and nutritional intervention may prevent progression to frailty
options.B: A score of 2 means he is 'frail' — immediate nursing home placement is indicated
options.C: A score of 2 means he is 'robust' — no interventions are needed
options.D: Fried Criteria cannot be applied to men — it was validated in women only

questions.rationale: Fried Frailty Criteria (5 components: weight loss, exhaustion, weakness, slow gait, low activity): 0 = robust, 1-2 = pre-frail, ≥3 = frail. This patient with 2 criteria is PRE-FRAIL — he has elevated risk for progression to frailty, adverse outcomes, and disability but is not yet frail. Pre-frailty is a critical intervention window: resistance exercise (most evidence-based frailty intervention), nutritional optimization (especially protein intake), and management of specific deficits (treat exhaustion's cause, address gait issues) can prevent or reverse pre-frailty progression. Nursing home placement (B) is not indicated by pre-frailty alone. He is NOT robust (C) — 2 criteria identifies abnormal. Fried Criteria were validated in both men and women in the Cardiovascular Health Study (D is false).

incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Score of 2 = pre-frail, not frail; nursing home placement is not clinically indicated by pre-frailty alone.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: 2 criteria = pre-frail, not robust — intervention is needed.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Fried Criteria were validated in both sexes in the Cardiovascular Health Study (>5,000 participants).

questions.keyConceptTested: Fried Frailty Criteria: 0=robust, 1-2=pre-frail, ≥3=frail; pre-frailty is actionable with exercise and nutritional intervention

questions.stem: An 80-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation on apixaban, hypertension, and moderate dementia (MoCA 17) falls and sustains a scalp laceration but no bone fracture. After wound care, which is the MOST nuanced next step?

options.A: Stop apixaban permanently — the fall confirms anticoagulation is too dangerous
options.B: Continue apixaban without change — risk of stopping exceeds fall risk in AF patients
options.C: Engage a structured risk-benefit conversation with patient and family: AF stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc calculation) vs. documented bleeding risk from fall; consider HASBLED score; optimize fall prevention; individualize decision with goals of care
options.D: Switch to aspirin — lower bleeding risk than anticoagulation with similar stroke prevention

questions.rationale: This is one of the highest-stakes prescribing decisions in geriatrics: anticoagulation in a demented older adult with documented falls and AF. There is no algorithmic answer — this requires individualized shared decision-making. Tools: CHA₂DS₂-VASc score (calculate stroke risk per year), HAS-BLED score (assess modifiable bleeding risk factors). Key evidence: in most patients with AF, the absolute stroke risk reduction from anticoagulation EXCEEDS the absolute fall risk (it would take approximately 295 falls/year to equal the harm from one ischemic stroke). However, dementia introduces adherence concerns, and unwitnessed falls that could cause intracranial hemorrhage are of concern. The decision should involve the patient (if decision-making capable), family/healthcare proxy, and goals of care preferences — not made unilaterally. Simply stopping (A) may expose her to stroke. Continuing unchanged (B) without fall prevention optimization is incomplete. Aspirin (D) is NOT equivalent to anticoagulation for AF stroke prevention — it provides significantly less protection and has similar bleeding risk.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Stopping anticoagulation increases stroke risk substantially — cannot be decided unilaterally based on one fall.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Optimizing fall prevention and having a goals-of-care conversation is needed — 'continue unchanged' is an incomplete response.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Aspirin does not adequately prevent AF-related stroke and has comparable bleeding risk — it is not a substitute for anticoagulation in AF.

questions.keyConceptTested: Anticoagulation + falls in demented elderly: individualized shared decision-making using CHA₂DS₂-VASc and HAS-BLED; aspirin is NOT equivalent to anticoagulation

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and CKD stage 3a (eGFR 52) is on metformin 1000 mg BID, sitagliptin 100 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg, and atorvastatin 40 mg. During a medication review, which Beers Criteria concern is MOST clinically significant?

options.A: Atorvastatin — statins should not be used in patients over 75 per Beers Criteria
options.B: Sitagliptin — dose requires adjustment for eGFR 52 (should be 50 mg/day at CrCl 30-50)
options.C: Metformin — absolutely contraindicated at eGFR <60 and must be stopped immediately
options.D: Lisinopril — ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in all diabetics over 70

questions.rationale: Sitagliptin (Januvia) requires renal dose adjustment: 100 mg daily for CrCl ≥50 mL/min; 50 mg daily for CrCl 30-49; 25 mg daily for CrCl <30. With eGFR 52, the patient is approaching the threshold where dose reduction to 50 mg is indicated (CrCl 30-50 = 50 mg). Checking actual CrCl (Cockcroft-Gault using actual body weight) is important in elderly — serum creatinine may not reflect true GFR. Atorvastatin (A) is not a Beers Criteria concern — statins are generally appropriate in older adults with established ASCVD risk; they are Beers Criteria 'use with caution' only in the absence of any ASCVD or CKD. Metformin (C) is contraindicated at eGFR <30, not <60 — at eGFR 52, use with caution and recheck periodically. Lisinopril (D) has NO Beers Criteria restriction in elderly diabetics — it is actually indicated for renal protection in diabetic nephropathy.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Statins are not Beers Criteria to avoid in older adults with established cardiovascular risk — this statement is inaccurate.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Metformin can be used with caution down to eGFR 30; contraindicated at <30, not <60.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: ACE inhibitors are indicated in diabetics for renal protection — not contraindicated in older adults.

questions.keyConceptTested: Sitagliptin renal dosing: 50 mg for CrCl 30-50; 25 mg for CrCl <30 — check Cockcroft-Gault in elderly where SCr underestimates CKD

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with multiple chronic conditions is being treated with amlodipine 10 mg for hypertension. Her daughter calls to report that her mother started taking diphenhydramine (Benadryl) PM nightly 3 weeks ago for sleep, purchased OTC. The daughter reports her mother has developed mild confusion and constipation over the past 2 weeks and had one fall. The most likely explanation is:

options.A: Early dementia — cognitive decline is expected at her age
options.B: Anticholinergic side effects from diphenhydramine — confusion and constipation are classic anticholinergic toxicity in older adults
options.C: Calcium channel blocker toxicity from amlodipine — constipation is a common side effect
options.D: Drug interaction between amlodipine and diphenhydramine causing excess CNS sedation

questions.rationale: This is a classic cascade prescribing/OTC medication toxicity scenario. Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) is a potent anticholinergic antihistamine — it is specifically listed in the Beers Criteria as a drug to AVOID in older adults. Anticholinergic effects in older adults: confusion/delirium (due to reduced central cholinergic reserve with aging), constipation (GI hypomotility), urinary retention, dry mouth, blurred vision, and fall risk. The timeline is perfect — symptoms began 2 weeks into OTC use. The action: stop diphenhydramine immediately; advise CBT-I or low-dose melatonin instead. Amlodipine (C) can cause constipation (calcium channel blocker GI effect) but does not cause confusion — cannot explain the cognitive changes. Dementia (A) does not develop over 2 weeks. There is no significant pharmacokinetic interaction (D) specifically between amlodipine and diphenhydramine causing CNS toxicity.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: 2-week onset rules out new dementia development — this is acute, reversible toxicity.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Amlodipine can cause constipation but NOT confusion — it does not cross the blood-brain barrier significantly.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: No significant pharmacokinetic drug-drug interaction between amlodipine and diphenhydramine causes additive CNS toxicity.

questions.keyConceptTested: Diphenhydramine anticholinergic toxicity in elderly: confusion + constipation + falls; Beers Criteria — avoid all first-generation antihistamines in older adults

questions.stem: A 84-year-old woman with multiple falls, osteoporosis, and mild cognitive impairment scores a TUG of 18 seconds. She is started on a structured falls prevention program. Which of the following interventions has the STRONGEST evidence for reducing fall frequency in this population?

options.A: Hip protectors alone
options.B: Multi-component exercise program incorporating balance training and lower extremity strengthening
options.C: Vitamin D supplementation alone (without exercise)
options.D: Cognitive-behavioral therapy for fear of falling alone

questions.rationale: The Cochrane systematic review of fall prevention interventions shows that multi-component exercise programs (combining balance training, gait training, and lower extremity strengthening — e.g., Otago Exercise Program, Tai Chi) have the strongest evidence for reducing fall rate and fall risk in older adults. These programs reduce falls by 23-34% in community-dwelling older adults. Multi-component programs are superior to single-modality interventions. Hip protectors (A) reduce hip fracture risk IF worn consistently, but they do not reduce fall frequency — and compliance is poor. Vitamin D alone (C) is beneficial when deficiency is corrected (improves neuromuscular function) but insufficient as a standalone fall prevention strategy. CBT for fear of falling (D) addresses psychological barriers and can complement exercise but has weaker fall-rate reduction evidence when used alone.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Hip protectors reduce fracture severity but NOT fall frequency — different outcome.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Vitamin D correction is helpful but insufficient alone — must be combined with exercise.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: CBT for fear of falling is a useful adjunct but does not have strong fall-rate reduction evidence when used in isolation.

questions.keyConceptTested: Falls prevention: multi-component exercise (balance + strength) has strongest evidence for reducing fall frequency in community-dwelling elderly

questions.stem: You are calculating drug dosing for a 78-year-old man who weighs 62 kg and has a serum creatinine of 1.1 mg/dL. The eGFR from the lab report reads 68 mL/min/1.73m². You need to calculate his actual creatinine clearance for drug dosing. Which calculation is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Use the eGFR of 68 directly — it is equivalent to CrCl for dosing purposes
options.B: Use Cockcroft-Gault equation: [(140-age) × weight(kg)] / [72 × serum creatinine] — gives estimated CrCl appropriate for drug dosing in elderly
options.C: Use the MDRD equation — more accurate than Cockcroft-Gault in elderly
options.D: Use ideal body weight only, regardless of actual weight

questions.rationale: Drug dosing in older adults requires Cockcroft-Gault (CG) CrCl, NOT eGFR (CKD-EPI or MDRD). This distinction is clinically critical. Why: eGFR is normalized to body surface area (1.73 m²) for CKD staging purposes; it does not reflect actual drug elimination capacity, especially in elderly patients with reduced muscle mass. An elderly patient with a normal serum creatinine but low muscle mass may have a significantly lower actual CrCl than eGFR suggests. CG formula: CrCl = [(140 - age) × weight in kg] / (72 × SCr) × 0.85 for females. Most drug package inserts and FDA guidelines use CG CrCl for dosing thresholds. This patient's CG CrCl ≈ [(140-78) × 62] / [72 × 1.1] = 48 mL/min — significantly lower than the eGFR of 68, which would change dosing decisions for renally cleared drugs (e.g., sitagliptin 50 mg, not 100 mg; apixaban dose reduction considerations).

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: eGFR and CrCl are NOT equivalent for drug dosing — eGFR is body-surface-area normalized and overestimates function in low-muscle-mass elderly.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: MDRD is also body-surface-area normalized and has the same limitation as eGFR for drug dosing.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Ideal body weight may be used in morbidly obese patients for some drugs, but actual body weight or adjusted body weight is used in most CG calculations.

questions.keyConceptTested: Renal dosing in elderly: use Cockcroft-Gault CrCl — NOT eGFR; elderly patients with low muscle mass may have significantly lower CrCl than eGFR suggests

questions.stem: An 81-year-old woman with dementia, behavioral symptoms (agitation, restlessness), and insomnia is being managed in a memory care facility. Staff requests quetiapine 25 mg nightly to manage her behavioral disturbances. Which statement BEST reflects evidence-based prescribing guidance?

options.A: Quetiapine is the safest antipsychotic in dementia and can be prescribed routinely for behavioral symptoms
options.B: All antipsychotics carry an FDA black box warning for increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis; non-pharmacological behavioral interventions should be exhausted first; if medication is necessary after documented failure of alternatives, use lowest dose with clear indication, inform family of risk, and document consent
options.C: Lorazepam 0.5 mg PRN is a safer alternative to quetiapine for dementia-related agitation
options.D: Prescribe diphenhydramine for nighttime sedation — it is safer than antipsychotics

questions.rationale: All antipsychotics (including atypical agents like quetiapine, risperidone, olanzapine) carry an FDA black box warning: increased risk of death in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis. Antipsychotics increase risk of pneumonia, falls, stroke, and all-cause mortality. Current guidelines require: (1) non-pharmacological behavioral interventions FIRST (structured activities, music therapy, caregiver training, environmental modifications), (2) if medication becomes necessary after documented failure of alternatives, use the lowest effective dose with regular reassessment and family-informed consent. Quetiapine is NOT the 'safest' antipsychotic — all carry the same black box warning; quetiapine is sometimes used because of lower EPS profile but is not exempt from mortality risk. Lorazepam (C) — benzodiazepines are Beers Criteria and cause paradoxical agitation in dementia, fall risk, and respiratory depression — a worse choice. Diphenhydramine (D) — first-generation antihistamines cause delirium and cognitive worsening in dementia — absolutely contraindicated.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: No antipsychotic is 'safe' for routine use in dementia behavioral symptoms — all carry FDA black box mortality warning.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Benzodiazepines cause paradoxical agitation and falls in dementia — worse than cautious antipsychotic use.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Diphenhydramine causes delirium and worsens dementia — absolutely contraindicated in this population.

questions.keyConceptTested: Antipsychotics in dementia: FDA black box warning for increased mortality; non-pharmacological interventions first; lowest dose with consent if needed

questions.stem: A 76-year-old man has been on omeprazole 20 mg daily for 7 years, originally prescribed for occasional heartburn. He has no active GERD symptoms, no H. pylori history, no NSAIDs, and no significant cardiac or GI history. Which is the MOST evidence-based approach?

options.A: Continue omeprazole indefinitely — PPIs are safe for long-term use in all elderly patients
options.B: Deprescribe omeprazole by stepping down to H2 receptor antagonist then stopping; counsel on lifestyle measures; restart only if symptoms return
options.C: Switch to pantoprazole — fewer drug interactions and safer for long-term use
options.D: Order endoscopy before stopping PPI — must rule out Barrett's esophagus first

questions.rationale: Long-term PPI use without a clear ongoing indication is a common prescribing cascade and overuse pattern. Risks of chronic PPI: Clostridioides difficile infection, fractures (bone density reduction), community-acquired pneumonia, hypomagnesemia, vitamin B12 malabsorption, CKD (observational association). This patient has no current indication for a PPI. Deprescribing is appropriate: step down (PPI → H2RA → discontinue) is preferred over abrupt stopping to avoid rebound acid hypersecretion. PPIs are NOT safe for indefinite use without indication — long-term risks are well-documented (A). Switching between PPIs (C) does not address the overuse. Endoscopy (D) is indicated if there were symptoms, history of Barrett's, or alarm features — not as a prerequisite for deprescribing a PPI in an asymptomatic patient without risk factors.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Long-term PPIs without indication carry documented risks — chronic use is not universally safe.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Switching PPIs does not address the underlying problem of overuse without indication.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Endoscopy is not required prior to deprescribing PPIs in an asymptomatic patient without alarm features or Barrett's history.

questions.keyConceptTested: PPI deprescribing: long-term PPI without indication should be stepped down; risks include C. diff, fractures, B12 deficiency, hypomagnesemia

questions.stem: An 88-year-old woman with severe aortic stenosis, moderate HFrEF (EF 35%), recurrent pneumonia, and advanced dementia is on atorvastatin 40 mg for primary prevention (no prior MI or stroke). Her daughter agrees the primary goal is quality of life. Which consideration BEST guides the decision about atorvastatin?

options.A: Statins must be continued lifelong once started — stopping increases cardiovascular event risk
options.B: In a patient with advanced dementia, severe AS, low life expectancy, and quality-of-life goals, deprescribing atorvastatin is appropriate — the NNT for primary prevention over remaining lifetime does not favor statin use in this context
options.C: Reduce atorvastatin to 10 mg — lower dose balances prevention with reduced side effects
options.D: Switch to rosuvastatin — more evidence-based in elderly with heart failure

questions.rationale: Deprescribing statins in patients with limited life expectancy and quality-of-life goals is an evidence-based, guideline-endorsed practice. In primary prevention (no prior MI/stroke), the number needed to treat (NNT) to prevent one cardiovascular event over 5 years is approximately 50-100 in moderate-risk populations. In an 88-year-old with severe AS, HFrEF, recurrent pneumonia, and advanced dementia with quality-of-life goals and likely limited life expectancy (<12-24 months), the expected time benefit from statin prevention will not be realized. STOPP criteria identify statin use in patients with estimated <12 months life expectancy as potentially inappropriate. The process: patient/family goals of care discussion, shared decision-making, explain the rationale for stopping, monitor for any symptoms after discontinuation. Continuing indefinitely (A) is not universally required — 'starting means lifelong' is a myth for primary prevention in this context. Dose reduction (C) doesn't address the fundamental question of whether any statin benefit applies here. Switching statin (D) doesn't address the appropriate indication question.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: 'Statins for life' is not a universal principle — deprescribing in limited life expectancy with quality-of-life goals is evidence-based.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Dose reduction without questioning the indication is an incomplete response to this clinical scenario.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Switching to a different statin does not address whether statin prevention is appropriate given limited life expectancy.

questions.keyConceptTested: Statin deprescribing in limited life expectancy: primary prevention NNT cannot be realized; goals-of-care discussion supports discontinuation

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man has been on diazepam 10 mg nightly for 8 years for anxiety and insomnia. You decide to begin deprescribing. Which approach is MOST appropriate?

options.A: Abruptly stop diazepam — he has been on it for 8 years and must stop urgently given fall risk
options.B: Gradually taper diazepam by 10% of current dose every 2-4 weeks while monitoring for withdrawal symptoms; overlap with non-pharmacological support
options.C: Switch to lorazepam — shorter-acting and safer for tapering in older adults
options.D: Add buspirone immediately to replace diazepam — equally effective and safer

questions.rationale: Benzodiazepine deprescribing after long-term use (>4 weeks) requires gradual tapering — abrupt cessation carries risk of severe withdrawal including seizures, delirium, and death. Diazepam has a very long half-life (20-100 hours, with active metabolites lasting 200+ hours) — this actually makes gradual tapering easier because the long half-life buffers against rapid concentration changes. The evidence-based protocol: 10% dose reduction every 2-4 weeks (some protocols use 5-10% every 1-2 weeks — slower is safer in elderly). Behavioral support (CBT-I, relaxation training) should be introduced concurrently to address underlying insomnia/anxiety. Switching to lorazepam (C) would substitute a short-acting benzo which is HARDER to taper and also Beers Criteria — incorrect. Abrupt stopping (A) risks seizures and life-threatening withdrawal in an 8-year user. Buspirone (D) is not equivalent to benzodiazepines — it has delayed onset (2-4 weeks), no cross-tolerance with benzos, and cannot substitute immediately.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Abrupt cessation after 8 years of benzodiazepine use risks severe withdrawal including seizures — never appropriate.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Lorazepam is shorter-acting with more frequent peaks — switching is not preferred; remain on diazepam for tapering.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Buspirone has no cross-tolerance with benzodiazepines and 2-4 week onset — it cannot immediately substitute and prevent withdrawal.

questions.keyConceptTested: Benzodiazepine tapering: 10% reduction every 2-4 weeks; never abrupt stop after long-term use; seizure risk with rapid withdrawal

questions.stem: A 73-year-old man with BPH and overactive bladder is prescribed oxybutynin 5 mg TID by urology for urinary urgency and frequency. His primary care NP reviews his medications and identifies significant concerns. What is the MOST appropriate action?

options.A: Continue oxybutynin — it was prescribed by urology and should not be altered in primary care
options.B: Contact urology to discuss switching to a more bladder-selective anticholinergic (solifenacin or darifenacin) with lower CNS penetration OR to a beta-3 agonist (mirabegron) — oxybutynin has high anticholinergic burden and Beers Criteria listing for older adults
options.C: Add donepezil to offset the anticholinergic cognitive effects of oxybutynin
options.D: Switch to tolterodine — same class but superior cognitive safety in all elderly patients

questions.rationale: Oxybutynin (particularly extended-release) has high CNS anticholinergic penetration — it causes cognitive impairment, confusion, and worsens dementia in older adults. It is specifically listed in the Beers Criteria as a drug to avoid in older adults. The NP's role in collaborative care includes identifying and addressing inappropriate prescribing from specialists. Appropriate action: contact urology and propose alternatives: (1) solifenacin or darifenacin — more bladder-selective, lower CNS penetration, but still anticholinergic; (2) mirabegron (Myrbetriq) — beta-3 agonist, no anticholinergic effects, evidence-based for overactive bladder, preferred in older adults. Adding donepezil (C) to offset anticholinergic effects is irrational polypharmacy — prescribing cascade. Tolterodine (D) is also a bladder anticholinergic — it has lower but not negligible CNS effects; still has Beers Criteria concerns; not superior to oxybutynin in all elderly patients. The preferred approach is to switch drug class, not trade one anticholinergic for another.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: NPs have a responsibility to address inappropriate prescribing regardless of originating specialty — polypharmacy review is a core NP function.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Adding donepezil to treat oxybutynin side effects is a prescribing cascade — stopping oxybutynin is the correct action.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Tolterodine is still an anticholinergic with CNS effects — mirabegron (beta-3 agonist) is the preferred non-anticholinergic alternative.

questions.keyConceptTested: Oxybutynin: Beers Criteria for older adults — high CNS anticholinergic burden; switch to mirabegron (beta-3 agonist) as preferred alternative

questions.stem: A 79-year-old woman with chronic low back pain has been taking ibuprofen 400 mg TID for 6 months prescribed by her orthopedist. She has stage 2 CKD (eGFR 62), takes lisinopril 10 mg for hypertension, and has a history of a GI bleed 5 years ago. What is the MOST appropriate response to this medication at her primary care visit?

options.A: Continue ibuprofen — the dose is low and she has been tolerating it
options.B: Switch to celecoxib 100 mg BID with a PPI — safer GI profile without changing the drug class
options.C: Deprescribe the NSAID; initiate acetaminophen 650-1000 mg TID scheduled + topical diclofenac gel for localized pain; refer to PT; contact orthopedist
options.D: Add misoprostol to prevent GI complications while continuing ibuprofen

questions.rationale: This patient has MULTIPLE contraindications to chronic NSAID use: (1) CKD stage 2 (NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandins → afferent arteriolar constriction → AKI, further CKD progression), (2) concomitant ACE inhibitor (triple whammy with NSAID + ACEi + any diuretic = high AKI risk), (3) prior GI bleed history, (4) age >65. Beers Criteria: NSAIDs in older adults with chronic kidney disease or prior GI bleed should be avoided. Celecoxib (B) reduces GI risk but still causes renal toxicity and blood pressure elevation — it is not an appropriate alternative in the setting of CKD + ACEi. Misoprostol (D) protects the GI mucosa but not the kidneys or cardiovascular system. The correct approach: deprescribe the NSAID, transition to acetaminophen (safe up to 3g/day in older adults without liver disease; adequate for musculoskeletal pain), add topical NSAIDs if localized pain (minimal systemic absorption), and communicate with the orthopedist about the contraindications.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Tolerance does not indicate safety — AKI, GI bleed, and cardiovascular risk accumulate with ongoing NSAID use.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Celecoxib still causes renal vasoconstriction — the COX-2 selectivity is GI, not renal; not appropriate in CKD + ACEi.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Misoprostol protects GI mucosa only — does not address renal or cardiovascular risk.

questions.keyConceptTested: NSAIDs in elderly with CKD + ACEi + prior GI bleed: Beers Criteria — deprescribe; replace with acetaminophen + topical NSAID; triple whammy AKI risk

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man presents with well-demarcated, salmon-pink plaques with thick silvery scale on his elbows, knees, and scalp. He also reports new-onset pain and swelling in his right second and third fingers (dactylitis). PASI score is 8. Which of the following is the MOST important next management step?

options.A: Start betamethasone dipropionate ointment and manage as mild-moderate plaque psoriasis
options.B: Refer urgently to rheumatology for suspected psoriatic arthritis; initiate a DMARD is likely needed regardless of PASI score
options.C: Start methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly — adequate for combined skin and joint disease
options.D: Reassure — dactylitis is not associated with psoriasis; treat the skin only

questions.rationale: Dactylitis (diffuse swelling of an entire digit, 'sausage finger') is pathognomonic for psoriatic arthritis (PsA). PsA affects ~30% of psoriasis patients and can cause irreversible joint destruction if untreated. The PASI score of 8 (mild-moderate skin disease) does not reflect the seriousness of joint involvement — PsA treatment decisions are driven by the joint disease severity, not just skin severity. Referral to rheumatology is the priority — DMARDs or biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-17/23 inhibitors) may be needed for joint protection. Topical steroids alone (A) are inadequate to treat or prevent PsA-related joint destruction. Methotrexate (C) can treat PsA but should be initiated in conjunction with rheumatology — it is not appropriate to start independently in primary care without specialist input for PsA. Dactylitis IS associated with psoriasis/PsA (D is factually incorrect).

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Topical steroids treat skin only — inadequate for active psoriatic arthritis with dactylitis.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: MTX requires specialist evaluation and monitoring; initiating without rheumatology input is not appropriate given the complexity.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Dactylitis is a hallmark feature of psoriatic arthritis — it must not be dismissed.

questions.keyConceptTested: Dactylitis in psoriasis patient = psoriatic arthritis until proven otherwise → rheumatology referral urgently; PASI score does not reflect PsA severity

questions.stem: A 5-year-old child (brought by parents) has diffuse, intensely itchy eczematous rash involving bilateral antecubital and popliteal fossae for 8 months. Skin shows thickening and lichenification from repeated scratching. Parents report sleep disruption. The child has a history of asthma and seasonal allergies. Which is the MOST appropriate first-line treatment approach?

options.A: Prescribe oral antihistamine (diphenhydramine) nightly — reduces itch and promotes sleep
options.B: Prescribe triamcinolone 0.1% ointment to all affected areas twice daily — strongest topical steroid first-line
options.C: Recommend daily emollient application (thick cream within 3 min of bath), moderate-potency topical corticosteroid for active lesions during flares, and avoidance of triggers; reserve TCI for eyelid and facial lesions
options.D: Refer immediately to dermatology for dupilumab initiation — lichenification indicates severe AD

questions.rationale: Atopic dermatitis management in children follows a stepwise approach with emollients as the absolute foundation for all severity levels. Correct approach: (1) daily emollients (soak-and-seal technique: bath then apply cream within 3 min), (2) moderate-potency topical corticosteroid (triamcinolone 0.1% or fluocinolone 0.01% cream) for trunk and extremities during active flares — using intermittent flare control, (3) TCI (tacrolimus 0.03% for children ≥2 years) for eyelid and facial lesions where steroids cause atrophy risk. Diphenhydramine (A) is not effective for AD itch (mechanism is not primarily histamine-mediated) and is Beers Criteria concern even in children for sedation/cognitive effect. Triamcinolone to ALL areas including face (B) would cause skin atrophy — high-potency steroids are not appropriate for facial use. Dupilumab referral (D) is appropriate for moderate-severe AD unresponsive to topical therapy — it is FDA-approved ≥6 months, but lichenification alone without failure of adequate topical therapy does not immediately warrant referral.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Diphenhydramine does not effectively treat AD pruritus — itch is not primarily histamine-mediated in AD; sedation risks in children are significant.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Triamcinolone to all areas including face causes atrophy; moderate-potency to body, low-potency or TCI to face.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Dupilumab referral is premature without an adequate trial of topical therapy; emollients + topical steroids are first-line.

questions.keyConceptTested: Atopic dermatitis management: emollients as foundation; topical CS for body flares; TCI for face/eyelids; diphenhydramine ineffective for AD itch

questions.stem: A patient with moderate plaque psoriasis (PASI 14, DLQI 12) has failed two courses of high-potency topical corticosteroids and one course of calcipotriol. He is not on any systemic therapy. What is the MOST appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Continue topical therapy with a different corticosteroid — try clobetasol propionate before considering systemic
options.B: Refer to dermatology for consideration of systemic therapy (MTX, cyclosporine) or biologic therapy based on disease severity and quality of life impact
options.C: Start methotrexate 7.5 mg weekly — straightforward treatment for moderate psoriasis
options.D: Prescribe emollient therapy only — systemic medications are too risky for this patient

questions.rationale: This patient meets biologic referral criteria: PASI >10 AND DLQI >10 (significant quality-of-life impact), with failure of ≥2 topical therapies. Current guidelines recommend referral to dermatology for systemic or biologic therapy at this threshold. The dermatologist will determine the most appropriate systemic agent (MTX, cyclosporine, or a biologic based on comorbidities, patient preference, and disease pattern). NPs should refer at the appropriate decision point rather than continuing inadequate topical escalation. Switching topicals (A) when multiple adequate topical courses have already failed is not evidence-based. Starting MTX independently (C) requires specialist oversight for dose adjustment, monitoring (LFTs, CBC), and contraindication screening — not appropriate to initiate independently in primary care without specialist co-management. Emollient only (D) is inadequate for PASI 14 — this is not mild disease.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: PASI 14 + DLQI 12 + failure of 2 topical agents = met referral criteria; more topicals without systemic therapy is inadequate.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: MTX initiation without specialist involvement is not appropriate — liver function monitoring and contraindication screening require dermatology oversight.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Emollient monotherapy is wholly inadequate for moderate-severe psoriasis.

questions.keyConceptTested: Biologic/systemic referral criteria: PASI >10 OR DLQI >10 + failure of ≥2 topical agents → dermatology referral
