questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman with moderate atopic dermatitis develops clusters of punched-out, crusted vesicular lesions on her face and neck over 48 hours, accompanied by fever (38.5°C) and worsening rash beyond her usual eczema distribution. She was not on topical steroids at the time. What is the MOST likely diagnosis and appropriate management?

options.A: Bacterial impetiginization — prescribe dicloxacillin orally
options.B: Eczema herpeticum (Kaposi varicelliform eruption) — start systemic acyclovir or valacyclovir; consider hospitalization if widespread or immunocompromised
options.C: Contact dermatitis flare — prescribe a high-potency topical steroid
options.D: Allergic reaction — prescribe oral prednisone 40 mg daily

questions.rationale: Eczema herpeticum is a life-threatening HSV superinfection of pre-existing atopic dermatitis. Classic features: punched-out, monomorphic vesicular/crusted lesions spreading rapidly beyond usual AD distribution, fever, and systemic illness in a patient with known AD. The disrupted skin barrier in AD provides HSV entry points. This is a dermatological emergency — systemic antiviral therapy is required: acyclovir 400 mg 5x/day OR valacyclovir 1 g TID. Hospitalization is indicated for: widespread disease, ocular involvement (risk of herpetic keratitis), immunocompromise, hemodynamic instability. Bacterial impetigo (A) presents with honey-crusted lesions — distinct morphology; punched-out vesicles with systemic fever = viral. High-potency topical steroid (C) would worsen HSV infection — steroids are immunosuppressive and contraindicated in active herpetic infection. Oral prednisone (D) would also worsen HSV — immune suppression is harmful.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Punched-out vesicles + fever in AD patient = eczema herpeticum; bacterial impetigo has different morphology and does not present this way.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Topical steroids are contraindicated in active HSV infection — they suppress local immunity and accelerate viral spread.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Oral corticosteroids are contraindicated in eczema herpeticum — immune suppression worsens HSV dissemination.

questions.keyConceptTested: Eczema herpeticum: HSV superinfection in AD — punched-out vesicles + fever = emergency; systemic acyclovir; steroids contraindicated

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with plaque psoriasis was started on methotrexate 15 mg PO weekly by dermatology 4 months ago. At today's primary care visit, you are managing his other conditions. His folic acid prescription has lapsed and he has not taken it in 6 weeks. Which monitoring or concern is MOST important to address today?

options.A: No concern — folic acid is optional for methotrexate-treated psoriasis
options.B: Restart folic acid 1 mg daily; check CBC and LFTs — folic acid reduces MTX side effects (mucositis, GI symptoms, hepatotoxicity); ensure monitoring is current
options.C: Stop methotrexate immediately — concurrent folic acid is mandatory or MTX is unsafe
options.D: Recommend a high-dose folic acid supplement (5 mg daily) — MTX requires higher folic acid doses than standard

questions.rationale: Folic acid supplementation (1 mg daily, or 5 mg once weekly on a non-MTX day in some guidelines) is strongly recommended with methotrexate therapy in psoriasis. Folic acid reduces MTX-related adverse effects: stomatitis/mucositis, nausea, hepatotoxicity, and myelosuppression. It does NOT significantly reduce MTX efficacy for psoriasis. MTX requires regular laboratory monitoring: CBC (risk of myelosuppression, macrocytosis), LFTs (hepatotoxicity) — monthly initially, then every 2-3 months in stable patients. Restarting folic acid and checking monitoring labs is the immediate priority. Stopping MTX (C) is not required — the lapse in folic acid is concerning but does not mandate immediate cessation; restarting folic acid and checking safety labs is appropriate. 5 mg daily (D) is higher than standard dosing — 1 mg daily is the standard for psoriasis (5 mg weekly is an alternative used in some protocols).

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Folic acid is not optional — it is a standard co-prescription with MTX to reduce toxicity.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: MTX does not need to be stopped for a 6-week folic acid lapse — restart folic acid and check safety labs.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: 5 mg daily is higher than standard — 1 mg/day or 5 mg/week (on non-MTX day) are the standard dosing options.

questions.keyConceptTested: Methotrexate monitoring: folic acid 1 mg/day required to reduce toxicity; CBC + LFTs every 2-3 months for ongoing monitoring

questions.stem: A 48-year-old male smoker (1.5 PPD x 20 years) presents for a routine visit. He says 'I know I should quit but I'm not ready.' Applying the 5A framework, what is the MOST appropriate action at this visit?

options.A: Skip the smoking discussion entirely — he is not ready and addressing it will damage the therapeutic relationship
options.B: Ask about tobacco, advise him clearly to quit, assess readiness, and apply the 5R framework (Relevance, Risks, Rewards, Roadblocks, Repetition) for a patient not yet ready to quit; document and revisit at next visit
options.C: Prescribe varenicline immediately — pharmacotherapy should start even without a quit date
options.D: Refer to a pulmonologist — smoking management is outside NP primary care scope

questions.rationale: The 5A framework applies at every visit even when patients are not ready to quit. For the 'not ready' patient, the specific intervention is motivational: Assess readiness → Apply 5R (Relevance: personalize why quitting matters; Risks: educate on health consequences; Rewards: benefits of quitting; Roadblocks: explore barriers; Repetition: return to topic at every visit). This approach has demonstrated efficacy in moving patients through the stages of change without confrontation. Skipping the discussion (A) misses a clinical mandate — brief NP counseling increases quit rates even for ambivalent patients. Prescribing varenicline (C) without a quit date or patient motivation is premature and reduces adherence (though some NPs will offer and patients may surprise themselves). Pulmonology referral (D) is not indicated — smoking cessation is a core NP primary care competency.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: The NP should always address tobacco at every visit — brief advice delivered non-judgmentally doubles quit rates.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Varenicline without patient readiness and a planned quit date has poor adherence outcomes — motivation is needed first.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Smoking cessation is a core NP primary care responsibility — referral is unnecessary.

questions.keyConceptTested: 5A + 5R framework for pre-contemplation stage smokers: Relevance, Risks, Rewards, Roadblocks, Repetition at every visit

questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with depression (currently managed with sertraline 150 mg daily) wants to quit smoking. She has tried nicotine patches twice without success. Which pharmacotherapy agent is MOST appropriate for her situation?

options.A: Bupropion SR 150 mg BID — dual benefit for both nicotine withdrawal and depression
options.B: Varenicline 1 mg BID — most effective cessation agent; safety in depression confirmed in EAGLES trial
options.C: High-dose nicotine patch 21 mg — increasing NRT dose resolves previous NRT failure
options.D: Nortriptyline — tricyclic antidepressant with evidence for smoking cessation in depression

questions.rationale: Varenicline (Chantix) is the most effective single pharmacological agent for smoking cessation — 2-3x higher quit rates than placebo, superior to bupropion and NRT monotherapy. The EAGLES trial (2016) is the landmark safety study: it specifically evaluated varenicline, bupropion, and NRT against placebo in patients WITH and WITHOUT psychiatric illness including depression. Results: no increased risk of neuropsychiatric events compared to placebo in any group, including those with depression. Prior concerns about neuropsychiatric safety were not confirmed. Varenicline is appropriate for patients with depression on SSRIs with appropriate monitoring. Bupropion (A) has dual benefit for depression and cessation but is second-line to varenicline for cessation efficacy; it is appropriate but not the MOST effective choice. Increasing NRT dose (C) alone rarely overcomes failure of 21 mg patch — adding a short-acting NRT (combination) or changing to varenicline is more evidence-based. Nortriptyline (D) has some cessation evidence but is rarely used due to anticholinergic side effects and safer alternatives.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Bupropion is a reasonable choice but varenicline has superior cessation efficacy; EAGLES trial cleared both in depression.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Increasing patch dose alone is not the best strategy after 2 patch failures — combination NRT or varenicline is more evidence-based.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Nortriptyline has evidence but is a second or third-line option; TCAs have more adverse effects and poorer tolerability.

questions.keyConceptTested: Varenicline most effective cessation agent; EAGLES trial cleared neuropsychiatric safety in depression — appropriate with monitoring

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with CKD stage 4 (CrCl 24 mL/min) wants to quit smoking. He smokes 2 PPD and is highly motivated. He asks about varenicline. Which is the MOST accurate prescribing information?

options.A: Varenicline is contraindicated in CKD stage 4 — use NRT only
options.B: Start varenicline at 0.5 mg daily (reduced from standard titration); maximum dose 0.5 mg BID for CrCl <30; monitor for efficacy and tolerability
options.C: Standard varenicline dosing 1 mg BID is safe at any level of renal function
options.D: Prescribe bupropion 150 mg BID — no dose adjustment needed in CKD

questions.rationale: Varenicline is renally cleared and requires dose adjustment in severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min). FDA recommendations: for CrCl <30 — start 0.5 mg daily and titrate to a maximum of 0.5 mg BID. Varenicline is NOT contraindicated in CKD — it can be used with appropriate dose reduction. Standard 1 mg BID dosing (C) in CrCl <30 would result in drug accumulation and increased adverse effects (nausea, neuropsychiatric). NRT is also safe in CKD but the patient is highly motivated and NRT had previously been inadequate — varenicline with dose adjustment is appropriate. Bupropion (D): while bupropion is primarily hepatically metabolized, its active metabolite (hydroxybupropion) is renally excreted — it also requires caution in severe renal impairment; not the preferred choice here given the clear NRT adjustment pathway for varenicline.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Varenicline is not contraindicated in CKD — it is approved with dose adjustment.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Standard dosing (1 mg BID) in CrCl <30 risks drug accumulation — dose reduction is required.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Bupropion's active metabolite is renally cleared — it also requires caution in severe CKD.

questions.keyConceptTested: Varenicline in CKD CrCl <30: reduce dose to max 0.5 mg BID; renally cleared — not contraindicated but requires adjustment

questions.stem: A patient starts bupropion SR for smoking cessation. She currently takes sertraline 100 mg daily. She is set to start bupropion 150 mg BID after 3 days at 150 mg daily. What is the MOST important drug interaction to monitor for?

options.A: QTc prolongation — both drugs prolong QT interval significantly
options.B: Serotonin syndrome — bupropion has significant serotonergic activity that interacts with sertraline
options.C: Seizure risk — bupropion lowers seizure threshold; sertraline may modestly affect seizure threshold; avoid twice-daily dosing less than 8 hours apart to maintain safety
options.D: Hypertensive crisis — bupropion with any SSRI causes hypertensive emergency

questions.rationale: Bupropion's primary safety concern is seizure risk — it lowers the seizure threshold in a dose-dependent manner. Key prescribing rules: (1) Maximum dose 450 mg/day total; (2) individual doses should not exceed 150 mg (for SR formulation) or 200 mg (XL) — taking doses too close together raises peak levels and seizure risk; (3) maintain at least 8 hours between SR doses; (4) avoid in patients with seizure disorder, eating disorders (bulimia/anorexia — electrolyte abnormalities lower seizure threshold), or those withdrawing from alcohol/benzos. Sertraline modestly affects seizure threshold — the combination is manageable but the primary concern remains bupropion dosing intervals. Bupropion does NOT cause significant QTc prolongation (A). Bupropion is NOT serotonergic — it inhibits dopamine and norepinephrine reuptake, NOT serotonin; serotonin syndrome risk with sertraline is negligible (B). Hypertensive crisis (D) is not a drug interaction between bupropion and SSRIs.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Bupropion does not significantly prolong QTc — this is not a major concern with this combination.
incorrectOptionExplanations.B: Bupropion inhibits dopamine and norepinephrine, not serotonin — serotonin syndrome with SSRIs is not a primary risk.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Hypertensive crisis is not a recognized interaction between bupropion and sertraline.

questions.keyConceptTested: Bupropion safety: seizure risk is the primary concern — maintain 8-hour interval between SR doses; avoid in seizure disorder and eating disorders

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman who is 14 weeks pregnant smokes 10 cigarettes/day and wants to quit but is worried about pharmacotherapy safety. She has tried to quit 3 times without medication. Which advice is MOST evidence-based?

options.A: Advise continued smoking — quitting in pregnancy can cause fetal distress from nicotine withdrawal
options.B: Offer nicotine replacement therapy (patch or gum) — NRT is preferred over continued smoking in pregnancy; discuss relative risks with patient
options.C: Start varenicline — most effective agent and safest in pregnancy
options.D: Start bupropion — approved for use in pregnancy at standard doses

questions.rationale: Smoking during pregnancy causes significant fetal harm: IUGR, placental abruption, preterm birth, stillbirth, SIDS. Quitting — even with NRT — is safer than continued smoking. NRT (patch, gum, lozenge) is the preferred pharmacotherapy option in pregnancy: nicotine is delivered without the 7,000 toxic compounds in cigarette smoke. Key counseling: NRT does not eliminate nicotine but dramatically reduces toxin exposure. The 16-hour patch (remove at night) is preferred to reduce overnight nicotine exposure. Intermittent forms (gum, lozenge) may be used for breakthrough cravings. Varenicline (C) has limited safety data in pregnancy — it is Category B (animal studies show no harm but no adequate human trials); most guidelines recommend against it in pregnancy given insufficient evidence; NRT is preferred. Bupropion (D) is also not routinely recommended — its safety in pregnancy is not established for the cessation indication. Option A is completely incorrect — continued smoking is clearly more harmful than NRT.

incorrectOptionExplanations.A: Continued smoking causes far greater harm than NRT — this advice is medically incorrect and potentially harmful.
incorrectOptionExplanations.C: Varenicline has insufficient pregnancy safety data — NRT is the preferred choice.
incorrectOptionExplanations.D: Bupropion is not routinely recommended in pregnancy for cessation — NRT is the preferred first-line option.

questions.keyConceptTested: Smoking cessation in pregnancy: NRT preferred over continued smoking; varenicline and bupropion not routinely recommended; NRT safer than smoking
